{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:12","agent_id":"a2af6e2e4","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:4","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"     1\t==========\n     2\tTime zones\n     3\t==========\n     4\t\n     5\t.. _time-zones-overview:\n     6\t\n     7\tOverview\n     8\t========\n     9\t\n    10\tWhen support for time zones is enabled, Django stores datetime information in\n    11\tUTC in the database, uses time-zone-aware datetime objects internally, and\n    12\ttranslates them to the end user's time zone in templates and forms.\n    13\t\n    14\tThis is handy if your users live in more than one time zone and you want to\n    15\tdisplay datetime information according to each user's wall clock.\n    16\t\n    17\tEven if your website is available in only one time zone, it's still good\n    18\tpractice to store data in UTC in your database. The main reason is daylight\n    19\tsaving time (DST). Many countries have a system of DST, where clocks are moved\n    20\tforward in spring and backward in autumn. If you're working in local time,\n    21\tyou're likely to encounter errors twice a year, when the transitions happen.\n    22\t(The pytz_ documentation discusses `these issues`_ in greater detail.) This\n    23\tprobably doesn't matter for your blog, but it's a problem if you over-bill or\n    24\tunder-bill your customers by one hour, twice a year, every year. The solution\n    25\tto this problem is to use UTC in the code and use local time only when\n    26\tinteracting with end users.\n    27\t\n    28\tTime zone support is disabled by default. To enable it, set :setting:`USE_TZ =\n    29\tTrue <USE_TZ>` in your settings file.\n    30\t\n    31\t.. note::\n    32\t\n    33\t    In Django 5.0, time zone support will be enabled by default.\n    34\t\n    35\tBy default, time zone support uses pytz_, which is installed when you install\n    36\tDjango; Django also supports the use of other time zone implementations like\n    37\t:mod:`zoneinfo` by passing :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` objects directly to\n    38\tfunctions in :mod:`django.utils.timezone`.\n    39\t\n    40\t.. versionchanged:: 3.2\n    41\t\n    42\t    Support for non-``pytz`` timezone implementations was added.\n    43\t\n    44\t.. note::\n    45\t\n    46\t    The default :file:`settings.py` file created by :djadmin:`django-admin\n    47\t    startproject <startproject>` includes :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>`\n    48\t    for convenience.\n    49\t\n    50\tIf you're wrestling with a particular problem, start with the :ref:`time zone\n    51\tFAQ <time-zones-faq>`.\n    52\t\n    53\tConcepts\n    54\t========\n    55\t\n    56\t.. _naive_vs_aware_datetimes:\n    57\t\n    58\tNaive and aware datetime objects\n    59\t--------------------------------\n    60\t\n    61\tPython's :class:`datetime.datetime` objects have a ``tzinfo`` attribute that\n    62\tcan be used to store time zone information, represented as an instance of a\n    63\tsubclass of :class:`datetime.tzinfo`. When this attribute is set and describes\n    64\tan offset, a datetime object is **aware**. Otherwise, it's **naive**.\n    65\t\n    66\tYou can use :func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware` and\n    67\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive` to determine whether datetimes are\n    68\taware or naive.\n    69\t\n    70\tWhen time zone support is disabled, Django uses naive datetime objects in local\n    71\ttime. This is sufficient for many use cases. In this mode, to obtain the\n    72\tcurrent time, you would write::\n    73\t\n    74\t    import datetime\n    75\t\n    76\t    now = datetime.datetime.now()\n    77\t\n    78\tWhen time zone support is enabled (:setting:`USE_TZ=True <USE_TZ>`), Django uses\n    79\ttime-zone-aware datetime objects. If your code creates datetime objects, they\n    80\tshould be aware too. In this mode, the example above becomes::\n    81\t\n    82\t    from django.utils import timezone\n    83\t\n    84\t    now = timezone.now()\n    85\t\n    86\t.. warning::\n    87\t\n    88\t    Dealing with aware datetime objects isn't always intuitive. For instance,\n    89\t    the ``tzinfo`` argument of the standard datetime constructor doesn't work\n    90\t    reliably for time zones with DST. Using UTC is generally safe; if you're\n    91\t    using other time zones, you should review the `pytz`_ documentation\n    92\t    carefully.\n    93\t\n    94\t.. note::\n    95\t\n    96\t    Python's :class:`datetime.time` objects also feature a ``tzinfo``\n    97\t    attribute, and PostgreSQL has a matching ``time with time zone`` type.\n    98\t    However, as PostgreSQL's docs put it, this type \"exhibits properties which\n    99\t    lead to questionable usefulness\".\n   100\t\n   101\t    Django only supports naive time objects and will raise an exception if you\n   102\t    attempt to save an aware time object, as a timezone for a time with no\n   103\t    associated date does not make sense.\n   104\t\n   105\t.. _naive-datetime-objects:\n   106\t\n   107\tInterpretation of naive datetime objects\n   108\t----------------------------------------\n   109\t\n   110\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, Django still accepts naive datetime\n   111\tobjects, in order to preserve backwards-compatibility. When the database layer\n   112\treceives one, it attempts to make it aware by interpreting it in the\n   113\t:ref:`default time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and raises a warning.\n   114\t\n   115\tUnfortunately, during DST transitions, some datetimes don't exist or are\n   116\tambiguous. In such situations, pytz_ raises an exception. That's why you should\n   117\talways create aware datetime objects when time zone support is enabled.\n   118\t\n   119\tIn practice, this is rarely an issue. Django gives you aware datetime objects\n   120\tin the models and forms, and most often, new datetime objects are created from\n   121\texisting ones through :class:`~datetime.timedelta` arithmetic. The only\n   122\tdatetime that's often created in application code is the current time, and\n   123\t:func:`timezone.now() <django.utils.timezone.now>` automatically does the\n   124\tright thing.\n   125\t\n   126\t.. _default-current-time-zone:\n   127\t\n   128\tDefault time zone and current time zone\n   129\t---------------------------------------\n   130\t\n   131\tThe **default time zone** is the time zone defined by the :setting:`TIME_ZONE`\n   132\tsetting.\n   133\t\n   134\tThe **current time zone** is the time zone that's used for rendering.\n   135\t\n   136\tYou should set the current time zone to the end user's actual time zone with\n   137\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.activate`. Otherwise, the default time zone is\n   138\tused.\n   139\t\n   140\t.. note::\n   141\t\n   142\t    As explained in the documentation of :setting:`TIME_ZONE`, Django sets\n   143\t    environment variables so that its process runs in the default time zone.\n   144\t    This happens regardless of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ` and of the\n   145\t    current time zone.\n   146\t\n   147\t    When :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this is useful to preserve\n   148\t    backwards-compatibility with applications that still rely on local time.\n   149\t    However, :ref:`as explained above <naive-datetime-objects>`, this isn't\n   150\t    entirely reliable, and you should always work with aware datetimes in UTC\n   151\t    in your own code. For instance, use :meth:`~datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp`\n   152\t    and set the ``tz`` parameter to :data:`~django.utils.timezone.utc`.\n   153\t\n   154\tSelecting the current time zone\n   155\t-------------------------------\n   156\t\n   157\tThe current time zone is the equivalent of the current :term:`locale <locale\n   158\tname>` for translations. However, there's no equivalent of the\n   159\t``Accept-Language`` HTTP header that Django could use to determine the user's\n   160\ttime zone automatically. Instead, Django provides :ref:`time zone selection\n   161\tfunctions <time-zone-selection-functions>`. Use them to build the time zone\n   162\tselection logic that makes sense for you.\n   163\t\n   164\tMost websites that care about time zones ask users in which time zone they live\n   165\tand store this information in the user's profile. For anonymous users, they use\n   166\tthe time zone of their primary audience or UTC. pytz_ provides helpers_, like a\n   167\tlist of time zones per country, that you can use to pre-select the most likely\n   168\tchoices.\n   169\t\n   170\tHere's an example that stores the current timezone in the session. (It skips\n   171\terror handling entirely for the sake of simplicity.)\n   172\t\n   173\tAdd the following middleware to :setting:`MIDDLEWARE`::\n   174\t\n   175\t    import pytz\n   176\t\n   177\t    from django.utils import timezone\n   178\t\n   179\t    class TimezoneMiddleware:\n   180\t        def __init__(self, get_response):\n   181\t            self.get_response = get_response\n   182\t\n   183\t        def __call__(self, request):\n   184\t            tzname = request.session.get('django_timezone')\n   185\t            if tzname:\n   186\t                timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(tzname))\n   187\t            else:\n   188\t                timezone.deactivate()\n   189\t            return self.get_response(request)\n   190\t\n   191\tCreate a view that can set the current timezone::\n   192\t\n   193\t    from django.shortcuts import redirect, render\n   194\t\n   195\t    def set_timezone(request):\n   196\t        if request.method == 'POST':\n   197\t            request.session['django_timezone'] = request.POST['timezone']\n   198\t            return redirect('/')\n   199\t        else:\n   200\t            return render(request, 'template.html', {'timezones': pytz.common_timezones})\n   201\t\n   202\tInclude a form in ``template.html`` that will ``POST`` to this view:\n   203\t\n   204\t.. code-block:: html+django\n   205\t\n   206\t    {% load tz %}\n   207\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   208\t    <form action=\"{% url 'set_timezone' %}\" method=\"POST\">\n   209\t        {% csrf_token %}\n   210\t        <label for=\"timezone\">Time zone:</label>\n   211\t        <select name=\"timezone\">\n   212\t            {% for tz in timezones %}\n   213\t            <option value=\"{{ tz }}\"{% if tz == TIME_ZONE %} selected{% endif %}>{{ tz }}</option>\n   214\t            {% endfor %}\n   215\t        </select>\n   216\t        <input type=\"submit\" value=\"Set\">\n   217\t    </form>\n   218\t\n   219\t.. _time-zones-in-forms:\n   220\t\n   221\tTime zone aware input in forms\n   222\t==============================\n   223\t\n   224\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django interprets datetimes entered in\n   225\tforms in the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` and returns\n   226\taware datetime objects in ``cleaned_data``.\n   227\t\n   228\tIf the current time zone raises an exception for datetimes that don't exist or\n   229\tare ambiguous because they fall in a DST transition (the timezones provided by\n   230\tpytz_ do this), such datetimes will be reported as invalid values.\n   231\t\n   232\t.. _time-zones-in-templates:\n   233\t\n   234\tTime zone aware output in templates\n   235\t===================================\n   236\t\n   237\tWhen you enable time zone support, Django converts aware datetime objects to\n   238\tthe :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` when they're rendered\n   239\tin templates. This behaves very much like :doc:`format localization\n   240\t</topics/i18n/formatting>`.\n   241\t\n   242\t.. warning::\n   243\t\n   244\t    Django doesn't convert naive datetime objects, because they could be\n   245\t    ambiguous, and because your code should never produce naive datetimes when\n   246\t    time zone support is enabled. However, you can force conversion with the\n   247\t    template filters described below.\n   248\t\n   249\tConversion to local time isn't always appropriate -- you may be generating\n   250\toutput for computers rather than for humans. The following filters and tags,\n   251\tprovided by the ``tz`` template tag library, allow you to control the time zone\n   252\tconversions.\n   253\t\n   254\t.. highlight:: html+django\n   255\t\n   256\tTemplate tags\n   257\t-------------\n   258\t\n   259\t.. templatetag:: localtime\n   260\t\n   261\t``localtime``\n   262\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   263\t\n   264\tEnables or disables conversion of aware datetime objects to the current time\n   265\tzone in the contained block.\n   266\t\n   267\tThis tag has exactly the same effects as the :setting:`USE_TZ` setting as far\n   268\tas the template engine is concerned. It allows a more fine grained control of\n   269\tconversion.\n   270\t\n   271\tTo activate or deactivate conversion for a template block, use::\n   272\t\n   273\t    {% load tz %}\n   274\t\n   275\t    {% localtime on %}\n   276\t        {{ value }}\n   277\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   278\t\n   279\t    {% localtime off %}\n   280\t        {{ value }}\n   281\t    {% endlocaltime %}\n   282\t\n   283\t.. note::\n   284\t\n   285\t    The value of :setting:`USE_TZ` isn't respected inside of a\n   286\t    ``{% localtime %}`` block.\n   287\t\n   288\t.. templatetag:: timezone\n   289\t\n   290\t``timezone``\n   291\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   292\t\n   293\tSets or unsets the current time zone in the contained block. When the current\n   294\ttime zone is unset, the default time zone applies.\n   295\t\n   296\t::\n   297\t\n   298\t    {% load tz %}\n   299\t\n   300\t    {% timezone \"Europe/Paris\" %}\n   301\t        Paris time: {{ value }}\n   302\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   303\t\n   304\t    {% timezone None %}\n   305\t        Server time: {{ value }}\n   306\t    {% endtimezone %}\n   307\t\n   308\t.. templatetag:: get_current_timezone\n   309\t\n   310\t``get_current_timezone``\n   311\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   312\t\n   313\tYou can get the name of the current time zone using the\n   314\t``get_current_timezone`` tag::\n   315\t\n   316\t    {% get_current_timezone as TIME_ZONE %}\n   317\t\n   318\tAlternatively, you can activate the\n   319\t:func:`~django.template.context_processors.tz` context processor and\n   320\tuse the ``TIME_ZONE`` context variable.\n   321\t\n   322\tTemplate filters\n   323\t----------------\n   324\t\n   325\tThese filters accept both aware and naive datetimes. For conversion purposes,\n   326\tthey assume that naive datetimes are in the default time zone. They always\n   327\treturn aware datetimes.\n   328\t\n   329\t.. templatefilter:: localtime\n   330\t\n   331\t``localtime``\n   332\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   333\t\n   334\tForces conversion of a single value to the current time zone.\n   335\t\n   336\tFor example::\n   337\t\n   338\t    {% load tz %}\n   339\t\n   340\t    {{ value|localtime }}\n   341\t\n   342\t.. templatefilter:: utc\n   343\t\n   344\t``utc``\n   345\t~~~~~~~\n   346\t\n   347\tForces conversion of a single value to UTC.\n   348\t\n   349\tFor example::\n   350\t\n   351\t    {% load tz %}\n   352\t\n   353\t    {{ value|utc }}\n   354\t\n   355\t.. templatefilter:: timezone\n   356\t\n   357\t``timezone``\n   358\t~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   359\t\n   360\tForces conversion of a single value to an arbitrary timezone.\n   361\t\n   362\tThe argument must be an instance of a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` subclass or a\n   363\ttime zone name.\n   364\t\n   365\tFor example::\n   366\t\n   367\t    {% load tz %}\n   368\t\n   369\t    {{ value|timezone:\"Europe/Paris\" }}\n   370\t\n   371\t.. highlight:: python\n   372\t\n   373\t.. _time-zones-migration-guide:\n   374\t\n   375\tMigration guide\n   376\t===============\n   377\t\n   378\tHere's how to migrate a project that was started before Django supported time\n   379\tzones.\n   380\t\n   381\tDatabase\n   382\t--------\n   383\t\n   384\tPostgreSQL\n   385\t~~~~~~~~~~\n   386\t\n   387\tThe PostgreSQL backend stores datetimes as ``timestamp with time zone``. In\n   388\tpractice, this means it converts datetimes from the connection's time zone to\n   389\tUTC on storage, and from UTC to the connection's time zone on retrieval.\n   390\t\n   391\tAs a consequence, if you're using PostgreSQL, you can switch between ``USE_TZ\n   392\t= False`` and ``USE_TZ = True`` freely. The database connection's time zone\n   393\twill be set to :setting:`TIME_ZONE` or ``UTC`` respectively, so that Django\n   394\tobtains correct datetimes in all cases. You don't need to perform any data\n   395\tconversions.\n   396\t\n   397\tOther databases\n   398\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   399\t\n   400\tOther backends store datetimes without time zone information. If you switch\n   401\tfrom ``USE_TZ = False`` to ``USE_TZ = True``, you must convert your data from\n   402\tlocal time to UTC -- which isn't deterministic if your local time has DST.\n   403\t\n   404\tCode\n   405\t----\n   406\t\n   407\tThe first step is to add :setting:`USE_TZ = True <USE_TZ>` to your settings\n   408\tfile. At this point, things should mostly work. If you create naive datetime\n   409\tobjects in your code, Django makes them aware when necessary.\n   410\t\n   411\tHowever, these conversions may fail around DST transitions, which means you\n   412\taren't getting the full benefits of time zone support yet. Also, you're likely\n   413\tto run into a few problems because it's impossible to compare a naive datetime\n   414\twith an aware datetime. Since Django now gives you aware datetimes, you'll get\n   415\texceptions wherever you compare a datetime that comes from a model or a form\n   416\twith a naive datetime that you've created in your code.\n   417\t\n   418\tSo the second step is to refactor your code wherever you instantiate datetime\n   419\tobjects to make them aware. This can be done incrementally.\n   420\t:mod:`django.utils.timezone` defines some handy helpers for compatibility\n   421\tcode: :func:`~django.utils.timezone.now`,\n   422\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_aware`,\n   423\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.is_naive`,\n   424\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_aware`, and\n   425\t:func:`~django.utils.timezone.make_naive`.\n   426\t\n   427\tFinally, in order to help you locate code that needs upgrading, Django raises\n   428\ta warning when you attempt to save a naive datetime to the database::\n   429\t\n   430\t    RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField ModelName.field_name received a naive\n   431\t    datetime (2012-01-01 00:00:00) while time zone support is active.\n   432\t\n   433\tDuring development, you can turn such warnings into exceptions and get a\n   434\ttraceback by adding the following to your settings file::\n   435\t\n   436\t    import warnings\n   437\t    warnings.filterwarnings(\n   438\t        'error', r\"DateTimeField .* received a naive datetime\",\n   439\t        RuntimeWarning, r'django\\.db\\.models\\.fields',\n   440\t    )\n   441\t\n   442\tFixtures\n   443\t--------\n   444\t\n   445\tWhen serializing an aware datetime, the UTC offset is included, like this::\n   446\t\n   447\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30+03:00\"\n   448\t\n   449\tWhile for a naive datetime, it isn't::\n   450\t\n   451\t    \"2011-09-01T13:20:30\"\n   452\t\n   453\tFor models with :class:`~django.db.models.DateTimeField`\\ s, this difference\n   454\tmakes it impossible to write a fixture that works both with and without time\n   455\tzone support.\n   456\t\n   457\tFixtures generated with ``USE_TZ = False``, or before Django 1.4, use the\n   458\t\"naive\" format. If your project contains such fixtures, after you enable time\n   459\tzone support, you'll see :exc:`RuntimeWarning`\\ s when you load them. To get\n   460\trid of the warnings, you must convert your fixtures to the \"aware\" format.\n   461\t\n   462\tYou can regenerate fixtures with :djadmin:`loaddata` then :djadmin:`dumpdata`.\n   463\tOr, if they're small enough, you can edit them to add the UTC offset that\n   464\tmatches your :setting:`TIME_ZONE` to each serialized datetime.\n   465\t\n   466\t.. _time-zones-faq:\n   467\t\n   468\tFAQ\n   469\t===\n   470\t\n   471\tSetup\n   472\t-----\n   473\t\n   474\t#. **I don't need multiple time zones. Should I enable time zone support?**\n   475\t\n   476\t   Yes. When time zone support is enabled, Django uses a more accurate model\n   477\t   of local time. This shields you from subtle and unreproducible bugs around\n   478\t   daylight saving time (DST) transitions.\n   479\t\n   480\t   When you enable time zone support, you'll encounter some errors because\n   481\t   you're using naive datetimes where Django expects aware datetimes. Such\n   482\t   errors show up when running tests. You'll quickly learn how to avoid invalid\n   483\t   operations.\n   484\t\n   485\t   On the other hand, bugs caused by the lack of time zone support are much\n   486\t   harder to prevent, diagnose and fix. Anything that involves scheduled tasks\n   487\t   or datetime arithmetic is a candidate for subtle bugs that will bite you\n   488\t   only once or twice a year.\n   489\t\n   490\t   For these reasons, time zone support is enabled by default in new projects,\n   491\t   and you should keep it unless you have a very good reason not to.\n   492\t\n   493\t#. **I've enabled time zone support. Am I safe?**\n   494\t\n   495\t   Maybe. You're better protected from DST-related bugs, but you can still\n   496\t   shoot yourself in the foot by carelessly turning naive datetimes into aware\n   497\t   datetimes, and vice-versa.\n   498\t\n   499\t   If your application connects to other systems -- for instance, if it queries\n   500\t   a web service -- make sure datetimes are properly specified. To transmit\n   501\t   datetimes safely, their representation should include the UTC offset, or\n   502\t   their values should be in UTC (or both!).\n   503\t\n   504\t   Finally, our calendar system contains interesting edge cases. For example,\n   505\t   you can't always subtract one year directly from a given date::\n   506\t\n   507\t       >>> import datetime\n   508\t       >>> def one_year_before(value):  # Wrong example.\n   509\t       ...     return value.replace(year=value.year - 1)\n   510\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 1, 10, 0))\n   511\t       datetime.datetime(2011, 3, 1, 10, 0)\n   512\t       >>> one_year_before(datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 29, 10, 0))\n   513\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   514\t       ...\n   515\t       ValueError: day is out of range for month\n   516\t\n   517\t   To implement such a function correctly, you must decide whether 2012-02-29\n   518\t   minus one year is 2011-02-28 or 2011-03-01, which depends on your business\n   519\t   requirements.\n   520\t\n   521\t#. **How do I interact with a database that stores datetimes in local time?**\n   522\t\n   523\t   Set the :setting:`TIME_ZONE <DATABASE-TIME_ZONE>` option to the appropriate\n   524\t   time zone for this database in the :setting:`DATABASES` setting.\n   525\t\n   526\t   This is useful for connecting to a database that doesn't support time zones\n   527\t   and that isn't managed by Django when :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``.\n   528\t\n   529\tTroubleshooting\n   530\t---------------\n   531\t\n   532\t#. **My application crashes with** ``TypeError: can't compare offset-naive``\n   533\t   ``and offset-aware datetimes`` **-- what's wrong?**\n   534\t\n   535\t   Let's reproduce this error by comparing a naive and an aware datetime::\n   536\t\n   537\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   538\t       >>> aware = timezone.now()\n   539\t       >>> naive = timezone.make_naive(aware)\n   540\t       >>> naive == aware\n   541\t       Traceback (most recent call last):\n   542\t       ...\n   543\t       TypeError: can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes\n   544\t\n   545\t   If you encounter this error, most likely your code is comparing these two\n   546\t   things:\n   547\t\n   548\t   - a datetime provided by Django -- for instance, a value read from a form or\n   549\t     a model field. Since you enabled time zone support, it's aware.\n   550\t   - a datetime generated by your code, which is naive (or you wouldn't be\n   551\t     reading this).\n   552\t\n   553\t   Generally, the correct solution is to change your code to use an aware\n   554\t   datetime instead.\n   555\t\n   556\t   If you're writing a pluggable application that's expected to work\n   557\t   independently of the value of :setting:`USE_TZ`, you may find\n   558\t   :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` useful. This function returns the current\n   559\t   date and time as a naive datetime when ``USE_TZ = False`` and as an aware\n   560\t   datetime when ``USE_TZ = True``. You can add or subtract\n   561\t   :class:`datetime.timedelta` as needed.\n   562\t\n   563\t#. **I see lots of** ``RuntimeWarning: DateTimeField received a naive\n   564\t   datetime`` ``(YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS)`` ``while time zone support is active``\n   565\t   **-- is that bad?**\n   566\t\n   567\t   When time zone support is enabled, the database layer expects to receive\n   568\t   only aware datetimes from your code. This warning occurs when it receives a\n   569\t   naive datetime. This indicates that you haven't finished porting your code\n   570\t   for time zone support. Please refer to the :ref:`migration guide\n   571\t   <time-zones-migration-guide>` for tips on this process.\n   572\t\n   573\t   In the meantime, for backwards compatibility, the datetime is considered to\n   574\t   be in the default time zone, which is generally what you expect.\n   575\t\n   576\t#. ``now.date()`` **is yesterday! (or tomorrow)**\n   577\t\n   578\t   If you've always used naive datetimes, you probably believe that you can\n   579\t   convert a datetime to a date by calling its :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date`\n   580\t   method. You also consider that a :class:`~datetime.date` is a lot like a\n   581\t   :class:`~datetime.datetime`, except that it's less accurate.\n   582\t\n   583\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   584\t\n   585\t       >>> import datetime\n   586\t       >>> import pytz\n   587\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   588\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   589\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   590\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   591\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   592\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   593\t       (True, False)\n   594\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   595\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   596\t       >>> paris\n   597\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   598\t       >>> new_york\n   599\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   600\t\n   601\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   602\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   603\t   fundamental.\n   604\t\n   605\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   606\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   607\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   608\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   609\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   610\t\n   611\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   612\t\n   613\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   614\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   615\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   616\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   617\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   618\t\n   619\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   620\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. Usually, this\n   621\t   will be the current timezone::\n   622\t\n   623\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   624\t       >>> timezone.activate(pytz.timezone(\"Asia/Singapore\"))\n   625\t       # For this example, we set the time zone to Singapore, but here's how\n   626\t       # you would obtain the current time zone in the general case.\n   627\t       >>> current_tz = timezone.get_current_timezone()\n   628\t       # Again, this is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   629\t       >>> local = current_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(current_tz))\n   630\t       >>> local\n   631\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 8, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Asia/Singapore' SGT+8:00:00 STD>)\n   632\t       >>> local.date()\n   633\t       datetime.date(2012, 3, 3)\n   634\t\n   635\t#. **I get an error** \"``Are time zone definitions for your database\n   636\t   installed?``\"\n   637\t\n   638\t   If you are using MySQL, see the :ref:`mysql-time-zone-definitions` section\n   639\t   of the MySQL notes for instructions on loading time zone definitions.\n   640\t\n   641\tUsage\n   642\t-----\n   643\t\n   644\t#. **I have a string** ``\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\"`` **and I know it's in the**\n   645\t   ``\"Europe/Helsinki\"`` **time zone. How do I turn that into an aware\n   646\t   datetime?**\n   647\t\n   648\t   This is exactly what pytz_ is for.\n   649\t\n   650\t       >>> from django.utils.dateparse import parse_datetime\n   651\t       >>> naive = parse_datetime(\"2012-02-21 10:28:45\")\n   652\t       >>> import pytz\n   653\t       >>> pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Helsinki\").localize(naive, is_dst=None)\n   654\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 2, 21, 10, 28, 45, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Helsinki' EET+2:00:00 STD>)\n   655\t\n   656\t   Note that ``localize`` is a pytz extension to the :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   657\t   API. Also, you may want to catch ``pytz.InvalidTimeError``. The\n   658\t   documentation of pytz contains `more examples`_. You should review it\n   659\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   660\t\n   661\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   662\t\n   663\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   664\t\n   665\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   666\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   667\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   668\t\n   669\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   670\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   671\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. So, in most circumstances,\n   672\t   the datetime in UTC returned by :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` will be\n   673\t   sufficient.\n   674\t\n   675\t   For the sake of completeness, though, if you really want the local time\n   676\t   in the current time zone, here's how you can obtain it::\n   677\t\n   678\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   679\t       >>> timezone.localtime(timezone.now())\n   680\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 20, 10, 53, 873365, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   681\t\n   682\t   In this example, the current time zone is ``\"Europe/Paris\"``.\n   683\t\n   684\t#. **How can I see all available time zones?**\n   685\t\n   686\t   pytz_ provides helpers_, including a list of current time zones and a list\n   687\t   of all available time zones -- some of which are only of historical\n   688\t   interest. :mod:`zoneinfo` also provides similar functionality via\n   689\t   :func:`zoneinfo.available_timezones`.\n   690\t\n   691\t.. _pytz: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/\n   692\t.. _more examples: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#example-usage\n   693\t.. _these issues: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#problems-with-localtime\n   694\t.. _helpers: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#helpers\n   695\t.. _tz database: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database\n","message":"695 lines read from file starting from line 1. Total lines in file: 695. End of file reached.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:12","agent_id":"a2af6e2e4","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.ReadFile:5","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"     1\t============\n     2\tDjango Utils\n     3\t============\n     4\t\n     5\t.. module:: django.utils\n     6\t   :synopsis: Django's built-in utilities.\n     7\t\n     8\tThis document covers all stable modules in ``django.utils``. Most of the\n     9\tmodules in ``django.utils`` are designed for internal use and only the\n    10\tfollowing parts can be considered stable and thus backwards compatible as per\n    11\tthe :ref:`internal release deprecation policy <internal-release-deprecation-policy>`.\n    12\t\n    13\t``django.utils.cache``\n    14\t======================\n    15\t\n    16\t.. module:: django.utils.cache\n    17\t   :synopsis: Helper functions for controlling caching.\n    18\t\n    19\tThis module contains helper functions for controlling HTTP caching. It does so\n    20\tby managing the ``Vary`` header of responses. It includes functions to patch\n    21\tthe header of response objects directly and decorators that change functions to\n    22\tdo that header-patching themselves.\n    23\t\n    24\tFor information on the ``Vary`` header, see :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.4`.\n    25\t\n    26\tEssentially, the ``Vary`` HTTP header defines which headers a cache should take\n    27\tinto account when building its cache key. Requests with the same path but\n    28\tdifferent header content for headers named in ``Vary`` need to get different\n    29\tcache keys to prevent delivery of wrong content.\n    30\t\n    31\tFor example, :doc:`internationalization </topics/i18n/index>` middleware would\n    32\tneed to distinguish caches by the ``Accept-language`` header.\n    33\t\n    34\t.. function:: patch_cache_control(response, **kwargs)\n    35\t\n    36\t    This function patches the ``Cache-Control`` header by adding all keyword\n    37\t    arguments to it. The transformation is as follows:\n    38\t\n    39\t    * All keyword parameter names are turned to lowercase, and underscores\n    40\t      are converted to hyphens.\n    41\t    * If the value of a parameter is ``True`` (exactly ``True``, not just a\n    42\t      true value), only the parameter name is added to the header.\n    43\t    * All other parameters are added with their value, after applying\n    44\t      ``str()`` to it.\n    45\t\n    46\t.. function:: get_max_age(response)\n    47\t\n    48\t    Returns the max-age from the response Cache-Control header as an integer\n    49\t    (or ``None`` if it wasn't found or wasn't an integer).\n    50\t\n    51\t.. function:: patch_response_headers(response, cache_timeout=None)\n    52\t\n    53\t    Adds some useful headers to the given ``HttpResponse`` object:\n    54\t\n    55\t    * ``Expires``\n    56\t    * ``Cache-Control``\n    57\t\n    58\t    Each header is only added if it isn't already set.\n    59\t\n    60\t    ``cache_timeout`` is in seconds. The :setting:`CACHE_MIDDLEWARE_SECONDS`\n    61\t    setting is used by default.\n    62\t\n    63\t.. function:: add_never_cache_headers(response)\n    64\t\n    65\t    Adds a ``Cache-Control: max-age=0, no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate,\n    66\t    private`` header to a response to indicate that a page should never be\n    67\t    cached.\n    68\t\n    69\t.. function:: patch_vary_headers(response, newheaders)\n    70\t\n    71\t    Adds (or updates) the ``Vary`` header in the given ``HttpResponse`` object.\n    72\t    ``newheaders`` is a list of header names that should be in ``Vary``. If\n    73\t    headers contains an asterisk, then ``Vary`` header will consist of a single\n    74\t    asterisk ``'*'``, according to :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.4`. Otherwise,\n    75\t    existing headers in ``Vary`` aren't removed.\n    76\t\n    77\t.. function:: get_cache_key(request, key_prefix=None, method='GET', cache=None)\n    78\t\n    79\t    Returns a cache key based on the request path. It can be used in the\n    80\t    request phase because it pulls the list of headers to take into account\n    81\t    from the global path registry and uses those to build a cache key to\n    82\t    check against.\n    83\t\n    84\t    If there is no headerlist stored, the page needs to be rebuilt, so this\n    85\t    function returns ``None``.\n    86\t\n    87\t.. function:: learn_cache_key(request, response, cache_timeout=None, key_prefix=None, cache=None)\n    88\t\n    89\t    Learns what headers to take into account for some request path from the\n    90\t    response object. It stores those headers in a global path registry so that\n    91\t    later access to that path will know what headers to take into account\n    92\t    without building the response object itself. The headers are named in\n    93\t    the ``Vary`` header of the response, but we want to prevent response\n    94\t    generation.\n    95\t\n    96\t    The list of headers to use for cache key generation is stored in the same\n    97\t    cache as the pages themselves. If the cache ages some data out of the\n    98\t    cache, this means that we have to build the response once to get at the\n    99\t    Vary header and so at the list of headers to use for the cache key.\n   100\t\n   101\t``django.utils.dateparse``\n   102\t==========================\n   103\t\n   104\t.. module:: django.utils.dateparse\n   105\t   :synopsis: Functions to parse strings to datetime objects.\n   106\t\n   107\tThe functions defined in this module share the following properties:\n   108\t\n   109\t- They accept strings in ISO 8601 date/time formats (or some close\n   110\t  alternatives) and return objects from the corresponding classes in Python's\n   111\t  :mod:`datetime` module.\n   112\t- They raise :exc:`ValueError` if their input is well formatted but isn't a\n   113\t  valid date or time.\n   114\t- They return ``None`` if it isn't well formatted at all.\n   115\t- They accept up to picosecond resolution in input, but they truncate it to\n   116\t  microseconds, since that's what Python supports.\n   117\t\n   118\t.. function:: parse_date(value)\n   119\t\n   120\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.date`.\n   121\t\n   122\t.. function:: parse_time(value)\n   123\t\n   124\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.time`.\n   125\t\n   126\t    UTC offsets aren't supported; if ``value`` describes one, the result is\n   127\t    ``None``.\n   128\t\n   129\t.. function:: parse_datetime(value)\n   130\t\n   131\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.datetime`.\n   132\t\n   133\t    UTC offsets are supported; if ``value`` describes one, the result's\n   134\t    ``tzinfo`` attribute is a :class:`datetime.timezone` instance.\n   135\t\n   136\t.. function:: parse_duration(value)\n   137\t\n   138\t    Parses a string and returns a :class:`datetime.timedelta`.\n   139\t\n   140\t    Expects data in the format ``\"DD HH:MM:SS.uuuuuu\"``,\n   141\t    ``\"DD HH:MM:SS,uuuuuu\"``,  or as specified by ISO 8601 (e.g.\n   142\t    ``P4DT1H15M20S`` which is equivalent to ``4 1:15:20``) or PostgreSQL's\n   143\t    day-time interval format (e.g. ``3 days 04:05:06``).\n   144\t\n   145\t``django.utils.decorators``\n   146\t===========================\n   147\t\n   148\t.. module:: django.utils.decorators\n   149\t    :synopsis: Functions that help with creating decorators for views.\n   150\t\n   151\t.. function:: method_decorator(decorator, name='')\n   152\t\n   153\t    Converts a function decorator into a method decorator. It can be used to\n   154\t    decorate methods or classes; in the latter case, ``name`` is the name\n   155\t    of the method to be decorated and is required.\n   156\t\n   157\t    ``decorator`` may also be a list or tuple of functions. They are wrapped\n   158\t    in reverse order so that the call order is the order in which the functions\n   159\t    appear in the list/tuple.\n   160\t\n   161\t    See :ref:`decorating class based views <decorating-class-based-views>` for\n   162\t    example usage.\n   163\t\n   164\t.. function:: decorator_from_middleware(middleware_class)\n   165\t\n   166\t    Given a middleware class, returns a view decorator. This lets you use\n   167\t    middleware functionality on a per-view basis. The middleware is created\n   168\t    with no params passed.\n   169\t\n   170\t    It assumes middleware that's compatible with the old style of Django 1.9\n   171\t    and earlier (having methods like ``process_request()``,\n   172\t    ``process_exception()``, and ``process_response()``).\n   173\t\n   174\t.. function:: decorator_from_middleware_with_args(middleware_class)\n   175\t\n   176\t    Like ``decorator_from_middleware``, but returns a function\n   177\t    that accepts the arguments to be passed to the middleware_class.\n   178\t    For example, the :func:`~django.views.decorators.cache.cache_page`\n   179\t    decorator is created from the ``CacheMiddleware`` like this::\n   180\t\n   181\t         cache_page = decorator_from_middleware_with_args(CacheMiddleware)\n   182\t\n   183\t         @cache_page(3600)\n   184\t         def my_view(request):\n   185\t             pass\n   186\t\n   187\t.. function:: sync_only_middleware(middleware)\n   188\t\n   189\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`synchronous-only <async-middleware>`. (The\n   190\t    default in Django, but this allows you to future-proof if the default ever\n   191\t    changes in a future release.)\n   192\t\n   193\t.. function:: async_only_middleware(middleware)\n   194\t\n   195\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`asynchronous-only <async-middleware>`. Django\n   196\t    will wrap it in an asynchronous event loop when it is called from the WSGI\n   197\t    request path.\n   198\t\n   199\t.. function:: sync_and_async_middleware(middleware)\n   200\t\n   201\t    Marks a middleware as :ref:`sync and async compatible <async-middleware>`,\n   202\t    this allows to avoid converting requests. You must implement detection of\n   203\t    the current request type to use this decorator. See :ref:`asynchronous\n   204\t    middleware documentation <async-middleware>` for details.\n   205\t\n   206\t``django.utils.encoding``\n   207\t=========================\n   208\t\n   209\t.. module:: django.utils.encoding\n   210\t   :synopsis: A series of helper functions to manage character encoding.\n   211\t\n   212\t.. function:: smart_str(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   213\t\n   214\t    Returns a ``str`` object representing arbitrary object ``s``. Treats\n   215\t    bytestrings using the ``encoding`` codec.\n   216\t\n   217\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   218\t    objects.\n   219\t\n   220\t.. function:: is_protected_type(obj)\n   221\t\n   222\t    Determine if the object instance is of a protected type.\n   223\t\n   224\t    Objects of protected types are preserved as-is when passed to\n   225\t    ``force_str(strings_only=True)``.\n   226\t\n   227\t.. function:: force_str(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   228\t\n   229\t    Similar to ``smart_str()``, except that lazy instances are resolved to\n   230\t    strings, rather than kept as lazy objects.\n   231\t\n   232\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   233\t    objects.\n   234\t\n   235\t.. function:: smart_bytes(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   236\t\n   237\t    Returns a bytestring version of arbitrary object ``s``, encoded as\n   238\t    specified in ``encoding``.\n   239\t\n   240\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   241\t    objects.\n   242\t\n   243\t.. function:: force_bytes(s, encoding='utf-8', strings_only=False, errors='strict')\n   244\t\n   245\t    Similar to ``smart_bytes``, except that lazy instances are resolved to\n   246\t    bytestrings, rather than kept as lazy objects.\n   247\t\n   248\t    If ``strings_only`` is ``True``, don't convert (some) non-string-like\n   249\t    objects.\n   250\t\n   251\t.. function:: iri_to_uri(iri)\n   252\t\n   253\t    Convert an Internationalized Resource Identifier (IRI) portion to a URI\n   254\t    portion that is suitable for inclusion in a URL.\n   255\t\n   256\t    This is the algorithm from section 3.1 of :rfc:`3987#section-3.1`, slightly\n   257\t    simplified since the input is assumed to be a string rather than an\n   258\t    arbitrary byte stream.\n   259\t\n   260\t    Takes an IRI (string or UTF-8 bytes) and returns a string containing the\n   261\t    encoded result.\n   262\t\n   263\t.. function:: uri_to_iri(uri)\n   264\t\n   265\t    Converts a Uniform Resource Identifier into an Internationalized Resource\n   266\t    Identifier.\n   267\t\n   268\t    This is an algorithm from section 3.2 of :rfc:`3987#section-3.2`.\n   269\t\n   270\t    Takes a URI in ASCII bytes and returns a string containing the encoded\n   271\t    result.\n   272\t\n   273\t.. function:: filepath_to_uri(path)\n   274\t\n   275\t    Convert a file system path to a URI portion that is suitable for inclusion\n   276\t    in a URL. The path is assumed to be either UTF-8 bytes, string, or a\n   277\t    :class:`~pathlib.Path`.\n   278\t\n   279\t    This method will encode certain characters that would normally be\n   280\t    recognized as special characters for URIs.  Note that this method does not\n   281\t    encode the ' character, as it is a valid character within URIs. See\n   282\t    ``encodeURIComponent()`` JavaScript function for more details.\n   283\t\n   284\t    Returns an ASCII string containing the encoded result.\n   285\t\n   286\t.. function:: escape_uri_path(path)\n   287\t\n   288\t    Escapes the unsafe characters from the path portion of a Uniform Resource\n   289\t    Identifier (URI).\n   290\t\n   291\t``django.utils.feedgenerator``\n   292\t==============================\n   293\t\n   294\t.. module:: django.utils.feedgenerator\n   295\t   :synopsis: Syndication feed generation library -- used for generating RSS, etc.\n   296\t\n   297\tSample usage::\n   298\t\n   299\t    >>> from django.utils import feedgenerator\n   300\t    >>> feed = feedgenerator.Rss201rev2Feed(\n   301\t    ...     title=\"Poynter E-Media Tidbits\",\n   302\t    ...     link=\"http://www.poynter.org/column.asp?id=31\",\n   303\t    ...     description=\"A group blog by the sharpest minds in online media/journalism/publishing.\",\n   304\t    ...     language=\"en\",\n   305\t    ... )\n   306\t    >>> feed.add_item(\n   307\t    ...     title=\"Hello\",\n   308\t    ...     link=\"http://www.holovaty.com/test/\",\n   309\t    ...     description=\"Testing.\",\n   310\t    ... )\n   311\t    >>> with open('test.rss', 'w') as fp:\n   312\t    ...     feed.write(fp, 'utf-8')\n   313\t\n   314\tFor simplifying the selection of a generator use ``feedgenerator.DefaultFeed``\n   315\twhich is currently ``Rss201rev2Feed``\n   316\t\n   317\tFor definitions of the different versions of RSS, see:\n   318\thttps://web.archive.org/web/20110718035220/http://diveintomark.org/archives/2004/02/04/incompatible-rss\n   319\t\n   320\t.. function:: get_tag_uri(url, date)\n   321\t\n   322\t    Creates a TagURI.\n   323\t\n   324\t    See https://web.archive.org/web/20110514113830/http://diveintomark.org/archives/2004/05/28/howto-atom-id\n   325\t\n   326\t``SyndicationFeed``\n   327\t-------------------\n   328\t\n   329\t.. class:: SyndicationFeed\n   330\t\n   331\t    Base class for all syndication feeds. Subclasses should provide write().\n   332\t\n   333\t    .. method:: __init__(title, link, description, language=None, author_email=None, author_name=None, author_link=None, subtitle=None, categories=None, feed_url=None, feed_copyright=None, feed_guid=None, ttl=None, **kwargs)\n   334\t\n   335\t        Initialize the feed with the given dictionary of metadata, which applies\n   336\t        to the entire feed.\n   337\t\n   338\t        Any extra keyword arguments you pass to ``__init__`` will be stored in\n   339\t        ``self.feed``.\n   340\t\n   341\t        All parameters should be strings, except ``categories``, which should\n   342\t        be a sequence of strings.\n   343\t\n   344\t    .. method:: add_item(title, link, description, author_email=None, author_name=None, author_link=None, pubdate=None, comments=None, unique_id=None, categories=(), item_copyright=None, ttl=None, updateddate=None, enclosures=None, **kwargs)\n   345\t\n   346\t        Adds an item to the feed. All args are expected to be strings except\n   347\t        ``pubdate`` and ``updateddate``, which are ``datetime.datetime``\n   348\t        objects, and ``enclosures``, which is a list of ``Enclosure`` instances.\n   349\t\n   350\t    .. method:: num_items()\n   351\t\n   352\t    .. method:: root_attributes()\n   353\t\n   354\t        Return extra attributes to place on the root (i.e. feed/channel)\n   355\t        element. Called from ``write()``.\n   356\t\n   357\t    .. method:: add_root_elements(handler)\n   358\t\n   359\t        Add elements in the root (i.e. feed/channel) element.\n   360\t        Called from ``write()``.\n   361\t\n   362\t    .. method:: item_attributes(item)\n   363\t\n   364\t        Return extra attributes to place on each item (i.e. item/entry)\n   365\t        element.\n   366\t\n   367\t    .. method:: add_item_elements(handler, item)\n   368\t\n   369\t        Add elements on each item (i.e. item/entry) element.\n   370\t\n   371\t    .. method:: write(outfile, encoding)\n   372\t\n   373\t        Outputs the feed in the given encoding to ``outfile``, which is a\n   374\t        file-like object. Subclasses should override this.\n   375\t\n   376\t    .. method:: writeString(encoding)\n   377\t\n   378\t        Returns the feed in the given encoding as a string.\n   379\t\n   380\t    .. method:: latest_post_date()\n   381\t\n   382\t        Returns the latest ``pubdate`` or ``updateddate`` for all items in the\n   383\t        feed. If no items have either of these attributes this returns the\n   384\t        current UTC date/time.\n   385\t\n   386\t``Enclosure``\n   387\t-------------\n   388\t\n   389\t.. class:: Enclosure\n   390\t\n   391\t    Represents an RSS enclosure\n   392\t\n   393\t``RssFeed``\n   394\t-----------\n   395\t\n   396\t.. class:: RssFeed(SyndicationFeed)\n   397\t\n   398\t``Rss201rev2Feed``\n   399\t------------------\n   400\t\n   401\t.. class:: Rss201rev2Feed(RssFeed)\n   402\t\n   403\t    Spec: https://cyber.harvard.edu/rss/rss.html\n   404\t\n   405\t``RssUserland091Feed``\n   406\t----------------------\n   407\t\n   408\t.. class:: RssUserland091Feed(RssFeed)\n   409\t\n   410\t    Spec: http://backend.userland.com/rss091\n   411\t\n   412\t``Atom1Feed``\n   413\t-------------\n   414\t\n   415\t.. class:: Atom1Feed(SyndicationFeed)\n   416\t\n   417\t    Spec: :rfc:`4287`\n   418\t\n   419\t``django.utils.functional``\n   420\t===========================\n   421\t\n   422\t.. module:: django.utils.functional\n   423\t    :synopsis: Functional programming tools.\n   424\t\n   425\t.. class:: cached_property(func, name=None)\n   426\t\n   427\t    The ``@cached_property`` decorator caches the result of a method with a\n   428\t    single ``self`` argument as a property. The cached result will persist\n   429\t    as long as the instance does, so if the instance is passed around and the\n   430\t    function subsequently invoked, the cached result will be returned.\n   431\t\n   432\t    Consider a typical case, where a view might need to call a model's method\n   433\t    to perform some computation, before placing the model instance into the\n   434\t    context, where the template might invoke the method once more::\n   435\t\n   436\t        # the model\n   437\t        class Person(models.Model):\n   438\t\n   439\t            def friends(self):\n   440\t                # expensive computation\n   441\t                ...\n   442\t                return friends\n   443\t\n   444\t        # in the view:\n   445\t        if person.friends():\n   446\t            ...\n   447\t\n   448\t    And in the template you would have:\n   449\t\n   450\t    .. code-block:: html+django\n   451\t\n   452\t        {% for friend in person.friends %}\n   453\t\n   454\t    Here, ``friends()`` will be called twice. Since the instance ``person`` in\n   455\t    the view and the template are the same, decorating the ``friends()`` method\n   456\t    with ``@cached_property`` can avoid that::\n   457\t\n   458\t        from django.utils.functional import cached_property\n   459\t\n   460\t        class Person(models.Model):\n   461\t\n   462\t            @cached_property\n   463\t            def friends(self):\n   464\t                ...\n   465\t\n   466\t    Note that as the method is now a property, in Python code it will need to\n   467\t    be accessed appropriately::\n   468\t\n   469\t        # in the view:\n   470\t        if person.friends:\n   471\t            ...\n   472\t\n   473\t    The cached value can be treated like an ordinary attribute of the instance::\n   474\t\n   475\t        # clear it, requiring re-computation next time it's called\n   476\t        del person.friends # or delattr(person, \"friends\")\n   477\t\n   478\t        # set a value manually, that will persist on the instance until cleared\n   479\t        person.friends = [\"Huckleberry Finn\", \"Tom Sawyer\"]\n   480\t\n   481\t    Because of the way the :py:ref:`descriptor protocol\n   482\t    <descriptor-invocation>` works, using ``del`` (or ``delattr``) on a\n   483\t    ``cached_property`` that hasn't been accessed raises ``AttributeError``.\n   484\t\n   485\t    As well as offering potential performance advantages, ``@cached_property``\n   486\t    can ensure that an attribute's value does not change unexpectedly over the\n   487\t    life of an instance. This could occur with a method whose computation is\n   488\t    based on ``datetime.now()``, or if a change were saved to the database by\n   489\t    some other process in the brief interval between subsequent invocations of\n   490\t    a method on the same instance.\n   491\t\n   492\t    You can make cached properties of methods. For example, if you had an\n   493\t    expensive ``get_friends()`` method and wanted to allow calling it without\n   494\t    retrieving the cached value, you could write::\n   495\t\n   496\t        friends = cached_property(get_friends)\n   497\t\n   498\t    While ``person.get_friends()`` will recompute the friends on each call, the\n   499\t    value of the cached property will persist until you delete it as described\n   500\t    above::\n   501\t\n   502\t        x = person.friends         # calls first time\n   503\t        y = person.get_friends()   # calls again\n   504\t        z = person.friends         # does not call\n   505\t        x is z                     # is True\n   506\t\n   507\t.. class:: classproperty(method=None)\n   508\t\n   509\t    Similar to :py:func:`@classmethod <classmethod>`, the ``@classproperty``\n   510\t    decorator converts the result of a method with a single ``cls`` argument\n   511\t    into a property that can be accessed directly from the class.\n   512\t\n   513\t.. function:: keep_lazy(func, *resultclasses)\n   514\t\n   515\t    Django offers many utility functions (particularly in ``django.utils``)\n   516\t    that take a string as their first argument and do something to that string.\n   517\t    These functions are used by template filters as well as directly in other\n   518\t    code.\n   519\t\n   520\t    If you write your own similar functions and deal with translations, you'll\n   521\t    face the problem of what to do when the first argument is a lazy\n   522\t    translation object. You don't want to convert it to a string immediately,\n   523\t    because you might be using this function outside of a view (and hence the\n   524\t    current thread's locale setting will not be correct).\n   525\t\n   526\t    For cases like this, use the ``django.utils.functional.keep_lazy()``\n   527\t    decorator. It modifies the function so that *if* it's called with a lazy\n   528\t    translation as one of its arguments, the function evaluation is delayed\n   529\t    until it needs to be converted to a string.\n   530\t\n   531\t    For example::\n   532\t\n   533\t        from django.utils.functional import keep_lazy, keep_lazy_text\n   534\t\n   535\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   536\t            # Do some conversion on string 's'\n   537\t            ...\n   538\t        fancy_utility_function = keep_lazy(str)(fancy_utility_function)\n   539\t\n   540\t        # Or more succinctly:\n   541\t        @keep_lazy(str)\n   542\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   543\t            ...\n   544\t\n   545\t    The ``keep_lazy()`` decorator takes a number of extra arguments (``*args``)\n   546\t    specifying the type(s) that the original function can return. A common\n   547\t    use case is to have functions that return text. For these, you can pass the\n   548\t    ``str`` type to ``keep_lazy`` (or use the :func:`keep_lazy_text` decorator\n   549\t    described in the next section).\n   550\t\n   551\t    Using this decorator means you can write your function and assume that the\n   552\t    input is a proper string, then add support for lazy translation objects at\n   553\t    the end.\n   554\t\n   555\t.. function:: keep_lazy_text(func)\n   556\t\n   557\t    A shortcut for ``keep_lazy(str)(func)``.\n   558\t\n   559\t    If you have a function that returns text and you want to be able to take\n   560\t    lazy arguments while delaying their evaluation, you can use this\n   561\t    decorator::\n   562\t\n   563\t        from django.utils.functional import keep_lazy, keep_lazy_text\n   564\t\n   565\t        # Our previous example was:\n   566\t        @keep_lazy(str)\n   567\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   568\t            ...\n   569\t\n   570\t        # Which can be rewritten as:\n   571\t        @keep_lazy_text\n   572\t        def fancy_utility_function(s, ...):\n   573\t            ...\n   574\t\n   575\t``django.utils.html``\n   576\t=====================\n   577\t\n   578\t.. module:: django.utils.html\n   579\t   :synopsis: HTML helper functions\n   580\t\n   581\tUsually you should build up HTML using Django's templates to make use of its\n   582\tautoescape mechanism, using the utilities in :mod:`django.utils.safestring`\n   583\twhere appropriate. This module provides some additional low level utilities for\n   584\tescaping HTML.\n   585\t\n   586\t.. function:: escape(text)\n   587\t\n   588\t    Returns the given text with ampersands, quotes and angle brackets encoded\n   589\t    for use in HTML. The input is first coerced to a string and the output has\n   590\t    :func:`~django.utils.safestring.mark_safe` applied.\n   591\t\n   592\t.. function:: conditional_escape(text)\n   593\t\n   594\t    Similar to ``escape()``, except that it doesn't operate on pre-escaped\n   595\t    strings, so it will not double escape.\n   596\t\n   597\t.. function:: format_html(format_string, *args, **kwargs)\n   598\t\n   599\t    This is similar to :meth:`str.format`, except that it is appropriate for\n   600\t    building up HTML fragments. All args and kwargs are passed through\n   601\t    :func:`conditional_escape` before being passed to ``str.format()``.\n   602\t\n   603\t    For the case of building up small HTML fragments, this function is to be\n   604\t    preferred over string interpolation using ``%`` or ``str.format()``\n   605\t    directly, because it applies escaping to all arguments - just like the\n   606\t    template system applies escaping by default.\n   607\t\n   608\t    So, instead of writing::\n   609\t\n   610\t        mark_safe(\"%s <b>%s</b> %s\" % (\n   611\t            some_html,\n   612\t            escape(some_text),\n   613\t            escape(some_other_text),\n   614\t        ))\n   615\t\n   616\t    You should instead use::\n   617\t\n   618\t        format_html(\"{} <b>{}</b> {}\",\n   619\t            mark_safe(some_html),\n   620\t            some_text,\n   621\t            some_other_text,\n   622\t        )\n   623\t\n   624\t    This has the advantage that you don't need to apply :func:`escape` to each\n   625\t    argument and risk a bug and an XSS vulnerability if you forget one.\n   626\t\n   627\t    Note that although this function uses ``str.format()`` to do the\n   628\t    interpolation, some of the formatting options provided by ``str.format()``\n   629\t    (e.g. number formatting) will not work, since all arguments are passed\n   630\t    through :func:`conditional_escape` which (ultimately) calls\n   631\t    :func:`~django.utils.encoding.force_str` on the values.\n   632\t\n   633\t.. function:: format_html_join(sep, format_string, args_generator)\n   634\t\n   635\t    A wrapper of :func:`format_html`, for the common case of a group of\n   636\t    arguments that need to be formatted using the same format string, and then\n   637\t    joined using ``sep``. ``sep`` is also passed through\n   638\t    :func:`conditional_escape`.\n   639\t\n   640\t    ``args_generator`` should be an iterator that returns the sequence of\n   641\t    ``args`` that will be passed to :func:`format_html`. For example::\n   642\t\n   643\t        format_html_join(\n   644\t            '\\n', \"<li>{} {}</li>\",\n   645\t            ((u.first_name, u.last_name) for u in users)\n   646\t        )\n   647\t\n   648\t.. function:: strip_tags(value)\n   649\t\n   650\t    Tries to remove anything that looks like an HTML tag from the string, that\n   651\t    is anything contained within ``<>``.\n   652\t\n   653\t    Absolutely NO guarantee is provided about the resulting string being\n   654\t    HTML safe. So NEVER mark safe the result of a ``strip_tag`` call without\n   655\t    escaping it first, for example with :func:`~django.utils.html.escape`.\n   656\t\n   657\t    For example::\n   658\t\n   659\t        strip_tags(value)\n   660\t\n   661\t    If ``value`` is ``\"<b>Joel</b> <button>is</button> a <span>slug</span>\"``\n   662\t    the return value will be ``\"Joel is a slug\"``.\n   663\t\n   664\t    If you are looking for a more robust solution, take a look at the `bleach\n   665\t    <https://pypi.org/project/bleach/>`_ Python library.\n   666\t\n   667\t.. function:: html_safe()\n   668\t\n   669\t    The ``__html__()`` method on a class helps non-Django templates detect\n   670\t    classes whose output doesn't require HTML escaping.\n   671\t\n   672\t    This decorator defines the ``__html__()`` method on the decorated class\n   673\t    by wrapping ``__str__()`` in :meth:`~django.utils.safestring.mark_safe`.\n   674\t    Ensure the ``__str__()`` method does indeed return text that doesn't\n   675\t    require HTML escaping.\n   676\t\n   677\t``django.utils.http``\n   678\t=====================\n   679\t\n   680\t.. module:: django.utils.http\n   681\t   :synopsis: HTTP helper functions. (URL encoding, cookie handling, ...)\n   682\t\n   683\t.. function:: urlencode(query, doseq=False)\n   684\t\n   685\t    A version of Python's :func:`urllib.parse.urlencode` function that can\n   686\t    operate on ``MultiValueDict`` and non-string values.\n   687\t\n   688\t.. function:: http_date(epoch_seconds=None)\n   689\t\n   690\t    Formats the time to match the :rfc:`1123#section-5.2.14` date format as\n   691\t    specified by HTTP :rfc:`7231#section-7.1.1.1`.\n   692\t\n   693\t    Accepts a floating point number expressed in seconds since the epoch in\n   694\t    UTC--such as that outputted by ``time.time()``. If set to ``None``,\n   695\t    defaults to the current time.\n   696\t\n   697\t    Outputs a string in the format ``Wdy, DD Mon YYYY HH:MM:SS GMT``.\n   698\t\n   699\t.. function:: base36_to_int(s)\n   700\t\n   701\t    Converts a base 36 string to an integer.\n   702\t\n   703\t.. function:: int_to_base36(i)\n   704\t\n   705\t    Converts a positive integer to a base 36 string.\n   706\t\n   707\t.. function:: urlsafe_base64_encode(s)\n   708\t\n   709\t    Encodes a bytestring to a base64 string for use in URLs, stripping any\n   710\t    trailing equal signs.\n   711\t\n   712\t.. function::  urlsafe_base64_decode(s)\n   713\t\n   714\t    Decodes a base64 encoded string, adding back any trailing equal signs that\n   715\t    might have been stripped.\n   716\t\n   717\t``django.utils.module_loading``\n   718\t===============================\n   719\t\n   720\t.. module:: django.utils.module_loading\n   721\t   :synopsis: Functions for working with Python modules.\n   722\t\n   723\tFunctions for working with Python modules.\n   724\t\n   725\t.. function:: import_string(dotted_path)\n   726\t\n   727\t    Imports a dotted module path and returns the attribute/class designated by\n   728\t    the last name in the path. Raises ``ImportError`` if the import failed. For\n   729\t    example::\n   730\t\n   731\t        from django.utils.module_loading import import_string\n   732\t        ValidationError = import_string('django.core.exceptions.ValidationError')\n   733\t\n   734\t    is equivalent to::\n   735\t\n   736\t        from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError\n   737\t\n   738\t``django.utils.safestring``\n   739\t===========================\n   740\t\n   741\t.. module:: django.utils.safestring\n   742\t   :synopsis: Functions and classes for working with strings that can be displayed safely without further escaping in HTML.\n   743\t\n   744\tFunctions and classes for working with \"safe strings\": strings that can be\n   745\tdisplayed safely without further escaping in HTML. Marking something as a \"safe\n   746\tstring\" means that the producer of the string has already turned characters\n   747\tthat should not be interpreted by the HTML engine (e.g. '<') into the\n   748\tappropriate entities.\n   749\t\n   750\t.. class:: SafeString\n   751\t\n   752\t    A ``str`` subclass that has been specifically marked as \"safe\" (requires no\n   753\t    further escaping) for HTML output purposes.\n   754\t\n   755\t.. function:: mark_safe(s)\n   756\t\n   757\t    Explicitly mark a string as safe for (HTML) output purposes. The returned\n   758\t    object can be used everywhere a string is appropriate.\n   759\t\n   760\t    Can be called multiple times on a single string.\n   761\t\n   762\t    Can also be used as a decorator.\n   763\t\n   764\t    For building up fragments of HTML, you should normally be using\n   765\t    :func:`django.utils.html.format_html` instead.\n   766\t\n   767\t    String marked safe will become unsafe again if modified. For example::\n   768\t\n   769\t        >>> mystr = '<b>Hello World</b>   '\n   770\t        >>> mystr = mark_safe(mystr)\n   771\t        >>> type(mystr)\n   772\t        <class 'django.utils.safestring.SafeString'>\n   773\t\n   774\t        >>> mystr = mystr.strip()  # removing whitespace\n   775\t        >>> type(mystr)\n   776\t        <type 'str'>\n   777\t\n   778\t``django.utils.text``\n   779\t=====================\n   780\t\n   781\t.. module:: django.utils.text\n   782\t    :synopsis: Text manipulation.\n   783\t\n   784\t.. function:: format_lazy(format_string, *args, **kwargs)\n   785\t\n   786\t    A version of :meth:`str.format` for when ``format_string``, ``args``,\n   787\t    and/or ``kwargs`` contain lazy objects. The first argument is the string to\n   788\t    be formatted. For example::\n   789\t\n   790\t        from django.utils.text import format_lazy\n   791\t        from django.utils.translation import pgettext_lazy\n   792\t\n   793\t        urlpatterns = [\n   794\t            path(format_lazy('{person}/<int:pk>/', person=pgettext_lazy('URL', 'person')),\n   795\t                 PersonDetailView.as_view()),\n   796\t        ]\n   797\t\n   798\t    This example allows translators to translate part of the URL. If \"person\"\n   799\t    is translated to \"persona\", the regular expression will match\n   800\t    ``persona/(?P<pk>\\d+)/$``, e.g. ``persona/5/``.\n   801\t\n   802\t.. function:: slugify(value, allow_unicode=False)\n   803\t\n   804\t    Converts a string to a URL slug by:\n   805\t\n   806\t    #. Converting to ASCII if ``allow_unicode`` is ``False`` (the default).\n   807\t    #. Converting to lowercase.\n   808\t    #. Removing characters that aren't alphanumerics, underscores, hyphens, or\n   809\t       whitespace.\n   810\t    #. Replacing any whitespace or repeated dashes with single dashes.\n   811\t    #. Removing leading and trailing whitespace, dashes, and underscores.\n   812\t\n   813\t    For example::\n   814\t\n   815\t        >>> slugify(' Joel is a slug ')\n   816\t        'joel-is-a-slug'\n   817\t\n   818\t    If you want to allow Unicode characters, pass ``allow_unicode=True``. For\n   819\t    example::\n   820\t\n   821\t        >>> slugify('你好 World', allow_unicode=True)\n   822\t        '你好-world'\n   823\t\n   824\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   825\t\n   826\t        In older versions, leading and trailing dashes and underscores are not\n   827\t        removed.\n   828\t\n   829\t.. _time-zone-selection-functions:\n   830\t\n   831\t``django.utils.timezone``\n   832\t=========================\n   833\t\n   834\t.. module:: django.utils.timezone\n   835\t    :synopsis: Timezone support.\n   836\t\n   837\t.. data:: utc\n   838\t\n   839\t    :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents UTC.\n   840\t\n   841\t.. function:: get_fixed_timezone(offset)\n   842\t\n   843\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents a time zone\n   844\t    with a fixed offset from UTC.\n   845\t\n   846\t    ``offset`` is a :class:`datetime.timedelta` or an integer number of\n   847\t    minutes. Use positive values for time zones east of UTC and negative\n   848\t    values for west of UTC.\n   849\t\n   850\t.. function:: get_default_timezone()\n   851\t\n   852\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents the\n   853\t    :ref:`default time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   854\t\n   855\t.. function:: get_default_timezone_name()\n   856\t\n   857\t    Returns the name of the :ref:`default time zone\n   858\t    <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   859\t\n   860\t.. function:: get_current_timezone()\n   861\t\n   862\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo` instance that represents the\n   863\t    :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   864\t\n   865\t.. function:: get_current_timezone_name()\n   866\t\n   867\t    Returns the name of the :ref:`current time zone\n   868\t    <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   869\t\n   870\t.. function:: activate(timezone)\n   871\t\n   872\t    Sets the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`. The\n   873\t    ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a :class:`~datetime.tzinfo`\n   874\t    subclass or a time zone name.\n   875\t\n   876\t.. function:: deactivate()\n   877\t\n   878\t    Unsets the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   879\t\n   880\t.. function:: override(timezone)\n   881\t\n   882\t    This is a Python context manager that sets the :ref:`current time zone\n   883\t    <default-current-time-zone>` on entry with :func:`activate()`, and restores\n   884\t    the previously active time zone on exit. If the ``timezone`` argument is\n   885\t    ``None``, the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>` is unset\n   886\t    on entry with :func:`deactivate()` instead.\n   887\t\n   888\t    ``override`` is also usable as a function decorator.\n   889\t\n   890\t.. function:: localtime(value=None, timezone=None)\n   891\t\n   892\t    Converts an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` to a different time zone,\n   893\t    by default the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   894\t\n   895\t    When ``value`` is omitted, it defaults to :func:`now`.\n   896\t\n   897\t    This function doesn't work on naive datetimes; use :func:`make_aware`\n   898\t    instead.\n   899\t\n   900\t.. function:: localdate(value=None, timezone=None)\n   901\t\n   902\t    Uses :func:`localtime` to convert an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` to a\n   903\t    :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date` in a different time zone, by default the\n   904\t    :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   905\t\n   906\t    When ``value`` is omitted, it defaults to :func:`now`.\n   907\t\n   908\t    This function doesn't work on naive datetimes.\n   909\t\n   910\t.. function:: now()\n   911\t\n   912\t    Returns a :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents the\n   913\t    current point in time. Exactly what's returned depends on the value of\n   914\t    :setting:`USE_TZ`:\n   915\t\n   916\t    * If :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``False``, this will be a\n   917\t      :ref:`naive <naive_vs_aware_datetimes>` datetime (i.e. a datetime\n   918\t      without an associated timezone) that represents the current time\n   919\t      in the system's local timezone.\n   920\t\n   921\t    * If :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True``, this will be an\n   922\t      :ref:`aware <naive_vs_aware_datetimes>` datetime representing the\n   923\t      current time in UTC. Note that :func:`now` will always return\n   924\t      times in UTC regardless of the value of :setting:`TIME_ZONE`;\n   925\t      you can use :func:`localtime` to get the time in the current time zone.\n   926\t\n   927\t.. function:: is_aware(value)\n   928\t\n   929\t    Returns ``True`` if ``value`` is aware, ``False`` if it is naive. This\n   930\t    function assumes that ``value`` is a :class:`~datetime.datetime`.\n   931\t\n   932\t.. function:: is_naive(value)\n   933\t\n   934\t    Returns ``True`` if ``value`` is naive, ``False`` if it is aware. This\n   935\t    function assumes that ``value`` is a :class:`~datetime.datetime`.\n   936\t\n   937\t.. function:: make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=None)\n   938\t\n   939\t    Returns an aware :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents the same\n   940\t    point in time as ``value`` in ``timezone``, ``value`` being a naive\n   941\t    :class:`~datetime.datetime`. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\n   942\t    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   943\t\n   944\t    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.AmbiguousTimeError`` exception is raised if\n   945\t    you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition where the same time\n   946\t    occurs twice (when reverting from DST). Setting ``is_dst`` to ``True`` or\n   947\t    ``False`` will avoid the exception by choosing if the time is\n   948\t    pre-transition or post-transition respectively.\n   949\t\n   950\t    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.NonExistentTimeError`` exception is raised\n   951\t    if you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition such that the\n   952\t    time never occurred. For example, if the 2:00 hour is skipped during a DST\n   953\t    transition, trying to make 2:30 aware in that time zone will raise an\n   954\t    exception. To avoid that you can use ``is_dst`` to specify how\n   955\t    ``make_aware()`` should interpret such a nonexistent time. If\n   956\t    ``is_dst=True`` then the above time would be interpreted as 2:30 DST time\n   957\t    (equivalent to 1:30 local time). Conversely, if ``is_dst=False`` the time\n   958\t    would be interpreted as 2:30 standard time (equivalent to 3:30 local time).\n   959\t\n   960\t    The ``is_dst`` parameter has no effect when using non-``pytz`` timezone\n   961\t    implementations.\n   962\t\n   963\t.. function:: make_naive(value, timezone=None)\n   964\t\n   965\t    Returns a naive :class:`~datetime.datetime` that represents in\n   966\t    ``timezone``  the same point in time as ``value``, ``value`` being an\n   967\t    aware :class:`~datetime.datetime`. If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\n   968\t    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\n   969\t\n   970\t``django.utils.translation``\n   971\t============================\n   972\t\n   973\t.. module:: django.utils.translation\n   974\t   :synopsis: Internationalization support.\n   975\t\n   976\tFor a complete discussion on the usage of the following see the\n   977\t:doc:`translation documentation </topics/i18n/translation>`.\n   978\t\n   979\t.. function:: gettext(message)\n   980\t\n   981\t    Translates ``message`` and returns it as a string.\n   982\t\n   983\t.. function:: pgettext(context, message)\n   984\t\n   985\t    Translates ``message`` given the ``context`` and returns it as a string.\n   986\t\n   987\t    For more information, see :ref:`contextual-markers`.\n   988\t\n   989\t.. function:: gettext_lazy(message)\n   990\t.. function:: pgettext_lazy(context, message)\n   991\t\n   992\t    Same as the non-lazy versions above, but using lazy execution.\n   993\t\n   994\t    See :ref:`lazy translations documentation <lazy-translations>`.\n   995\t\n   996\t.. function:: gettext_noop(message)\n   997\t\n   998\t    Marks strings for translation but doesn't translate them now.
