    1\t==================\n     2\tDatabase Functions\n     3\t==================\n     4\t\n     5\t.. module:: django.db.models.functions\n     6\t    :synopsis: Database Functions\n     7\t\n     8\tThe classes documented below provide a way for users to use functions provided\n     9\tby the underlying database as annotations, aggregations, or filters in Django.\n    10\tFunctions are also :doc:`expressions <expressions>`, so they can be used and\n    11\tcombined with other expressions like :ref:`aggregate functions\n    12\t<aggregation-functions>`.\n    13\t\n    14\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n    15\t\n    16\t    class Author(models.Model):\n    17\t        name = models.CharField(max_length=50)\n    18\t        age = models.PositiveIntegerField(null=True, blank=True)\n    19\t        alias = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    20\t        goes_by = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)\n    21\t\n    22\tWe don't usually recommend allowing ``null=True`` for ``CharField`` since this\n    23\tallows the field to have two \"empty values\", but it's important for the\n    24\t``Coalesce`` example below.\n    25\t\n    26\t.. _comparison-functions:\n    27\t\n    28\tComparison and conversion functions\n    29\t===================================\n    30\t\n    31\t``Cast``\n    32\t--------\n    33\t\n    34\t.. class:: Cast(expression, output_field)\n    35\t\n    36\tForces the result type of ``expression`` to be the one from ``output_field``.\n    37\t\n    38\tUsage example::\n    39\t\n    40\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n    41\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast\n    42\t    >>> Author.objects.create(age=25, name='Margaret Smith')\n    43\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    44\t    ...    age_as_float=Cast('age', output_field=FloatField()),\n    45\t    ... ).get()\n    46\t    >>> print(author.age_as_float)\n    47\t    25.0\n    48\t\n    49\t``Coalesce``\n    50\t------------\n    51\t\n    52\t.. class:: Coalesce(*expressions, **extra)\n    53\t\n    54\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n    55\tfirst non-null value (note that an empty string is not considered a null\n    56\tvalue). Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n    57\twill result in a database error.\n    58\t\n    59\tUsage examples::\n    60\t\n    61\t    >>> # Get a screen name from least to most public\n    62\t    >>> from django.db.models import Sum\n    63\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Coalesce\n    64\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', goes_by='Maggie')\n    65\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n    66\t    ...    screen_name=Coalesce('alias', 'goes_by', 'name')).get()\n    67\t    >>> print(author.screen_name)\n    68\t    Maggie\n    69\t\n    70\t    >>> # Prevent an aggregate Sum() from returning None\n    71\t    >>> # The aggregate default argument uses Coalesce() under the hood.\n    72\t    >>> aggregated = Author.objects.aggregate(\n    73\t    ...    combined_age=Sum('age'),\n    74\t    ...    combined_age_default=Sum('age', default=0),\n    75\t    ...    combined_age_coalesce=Coalesce(Sum('age'), 0),\n    76\t    ... )\n    77\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age'])\n    78\t    None\n    79\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_default'])\n    80\t    0\n    81\t    >>> print(aggregated['combined_age_coalesce'])\n    82\t    0\n    83\t\n    84\t.. warning::\n    85\t\n    86\t    A Python value passed to ``Coalesce`` on MySQL may be converted to an\n    87\t    incorrect type unless explicitly cast to the correct database type:\n    88\t\n    89\t    >>> from django.db.models import DateTimeField\n    90\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Cast, Coalesce\n    91\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n    92\t    >>> now = timezone.now()\n    93\t    >>> Coalesce('updated', Cast(now, DateTimeField()))\n    94\t\n    95\t``Collate``\n    96\t-----------\n    97\t\n    98\t.. class:: Collate(expression, collation)\n    99\t\n   100\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   101\t\n   102\tTakes an expression and a collation name to query against.\n   103\t\n   104\tFor example, to filter case-insensitively in SQLite::\n   105\t\n   106\t    >>> Author.objects.filter(name=Collate(Value('john'), 'nocase'))\n   107\t    <QuerySet [<Author: John>, <Author: john>]>\n   108\t\n   109\tIt can also be used when ordering, for example with PostgreSQL::\n   110\t\n   111\t    >>> Author.objects.order_by(Collate('name', 'et-x-icu'))\n   112\t    <QuerySet [<Author: Ursula>, <Author: Veronika>, <Author: Ülle>]>\n   113\t\n   114\t``Greatest``\n   115\t------------\n   116\t\n   117\t.. class:: Greatest(*expressions, **extra)\n   118\t\n   119\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   120\tgreatest value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and\n   121\tnumbers will result in a database error.\n   122\t\n   123\tUsage example::\n   124\t\n   125\t    class Blog(models.Model):\n   126\t        body = models.TextField()\n   127\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   128\t\n   129\t    class Comment(models.Model):\n   130\t        body = models.TextField()\n   131\t        modified = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True)\n   132\t        blog = models.ForeignKey(Blog, on_delete=models.CASCADE)\n   133\t\n   134\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Greatest\n   135\t    >>> blog = Blog.objects.create(body='Greatest is the best.')\n   136\t    >>> comment = Comment.objects.create(body='No, Least is better.', blog=blog)\n   137\t    >>> comments = Comment.objects.annotate(last_updated=Greatest('modified', 'blog__modified'))\n   138\t    >>> annotated_comment = comments.get()\n   139\t\n   140\t``annotated_comment.last_updated`` will be the most recent of ``blog.modified``\n   141\tand ``comment.modified``.\n   142\t\n   143\t.. warning::\n   144\t\n   145\t    The behavior of ``Greatest`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   146\t    varies between databases:\n   147\t\n   148\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Greatest`` will return the largest non-null expression,\n   149\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   150\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Greatest``\n   151\t      will return ``null``.\n   152\t\n   153\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   154\t    a sensible minimum value to provide as a default.\n   155\t\n   156\t``JSONObject``\n   157\t--------------\n   158\t\n   159\t.. class:: JSONObject(**fields)\n   160\t\n   161\t.. versionadded:: 3.2\n   162\t\n   163\tTakes a list of key-value pairs and returns a JSON object containing those\n   164\tpairs.\n   165\t\n   166\tUsage example::\n   167\t\n   168\t    >>> from django.db.models import F\n   169\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import JSONObject, Lower\n   170\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', alias='msmith', age=25)\n   171\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(json_object=JSONObject(\n   172\t    ...     name=Lower('name'),\n   173\t    ...     alias='alias',\n   174\t    ...     age=F('age') * 2,\n   175\t    ... )).get()\n   176\t    >>> author.json_object\n   177\t    {'name': 'margaret smith', 'alias': 'msmith', 'age': 50}\n   178\t\n   179\t``Least``\n   180\t---------\n   181\t\n   182\t.. class:: Least(*expressions, **extra)\n   183\t\n   184\tAccepts a list of at least two field names or expressions and returns the\n   185\tleast value. Each argument must be of a similar type, so mixing text and numbers\n   186\twill result in a database error.\n   187\t\n   188\t.. warning::\n   189\t\n   190\t    The behavior of ``Least`` when one or more expression may be ``null``\n   191\t    varies between databases:\n   192\t\n   193\t    - PostgreSQL: ``Least`` will return the smallest non-null expression,\n   194\t      or ``null`` if all expressions are ``null``.\n   195\t    - SQLite, Oracle, and MySQL: If any expression is ``null``, ``Least``\n   196\t      will return ``null``.\n   197\t\n   198\t    The PostgreSQL behavior can be emulated using ``Coalesce`` if you know\n   199\t    a sensible maximum value to provide as a default.\n   200\t\n   201\t``NullIf``\n   202\t----------\n   203\t\n   204\t.. class:: NullIf(expression1, expression2)\n   205\t\n   206\tAccepts two expressions and returns ``None`` if they are equal, otherwise\n   207\treturns ``expression1``.\n   208\t\n   209\t.. admonition:: Caveats on Oracle\n   210\t\n   211\t    Due to an :ref:`Oracle convention<oracle-null-empty-strings>`, this\n   212\t    function returns the empty string instead of ``None`` when the expressions\n   213\t    are of type :class:`~django.db.models.CharField`.\n   214\t\n   215\t    Passing ``Value(None)`` to ``expression1`` is prohibited on Oracle since\n   216\t    Oracle doesn't accept ``NULL`` as the first argument.\n   217\t\n   218\t.. _date-functions:\n   219\t\n   220\tDate functions\n   221\t==============\n   222\t\n   223\tWe'll be using the following model in examples of each function::\n   224\t\n   225\t    class Experiment(models.Model):\n   226\t        start_datetime = models.DateTimeField()\n   227\t        start_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   228\t        start_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   229\t        end_datetime = models.DateTimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   230\t        end_date = models.DateField(null=True, blank=True)\n   231\t        end_time = models.TimeField(null=True, blank=True)\n   232\t\n   233\t``Extract``\n   234\t-----------\n   235\t\n   236\t.. class:: Extract(expression, lookup_name=None, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   237\t\n   238\tExtracts a component of a date as a number.\n   239\t\n   240\tTakes an ``expression`` representing a ``DateField``, ``DateTimeField``,\n   241\t``TimeField``, or ``DurationField`` and a ``lookup_name``, and returns the part\n   242\tof the date referenced by ``lookup_name`` as an ``IntegerField``.\n   243\tDjango usually uses the databases' extract function, so you may use any\n   244\t``lookup_name`` that your database supports. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually\n   245\tprovided by ``pytz``, can be passed to extract a value in a specific timezone.\n   246\t\n   247\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 23:30:01.000321+00:00``, the built-in\n   248\t``lookup_name``\\s return:\n   249\t\n   250\t* \"year\": 2015\n   251\t* \"iso_year\": 2015\n   252\t* \"quarter\": 2\n   253\t* \"month\": 6\n   254\t* \"day\": 15\n   255\t* \"week\": 25\n   256\t* \"week_day\": 2\n   257\t* \"iso_week_day\": 1\n   258\t* \"hour\": 23\n   259\t* \"minute\": 30\n   260\t* \"second\": 1\n   261\t\n   262\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   263\tthe datetime is converted to the timezone before the value is extracted. The\n   264\ttimezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The values\n   265\treturned when this timezone is active will be the same as above except for:\n   266\t\n   267\t* \"day\": 16\n   268\t* \"week_day\": 3\n   269\t* \"iso_week_day\": 2\n   270\t* \"hour\": 9\n   271\t\n   272\t.. admonition:: ``week_day`` values\n   273\t\n   274\t    The ``week_day`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated differently from most\n   275\t    databases and from Python's standard functions. This function will return\n   276\t    ``1`` for Sunday, ``2`` for Monday, through ``7`` for Saturday.\n   277\t\n   278\t    The equivalent calculation in Python is::\n   279\t\n   280\t        >>> from datetime import datetime\n   281\t        >>> dt = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   282\t        >>> (dt.isoweekday() % 7) + 1\n   283\t        2\n   284\t\n   285\t.. admonition:: ``week`` values\n   286\t\n   287\t    The ``week`` ``lookup_type`` is calculated based on `ISO-8601\n   288\t    <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO-8601>`_, i.e.,\n   289\t    a week starts on a Monday. The first week of a year is the one that\n   290\t    contains the year's first Thursday, i.e. the first week has the majority\n   291\t    (four or more) of its days in the year. The value returned is in the range\n   292\t    1 to 52 or 53.\n   293\t\n   294\tEach ``lookup_name`` above has a corresponding ``Extract`` subclass (listed\n   295\tbelow) that should typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   296\te.g. use ``ExtractYear(...)`` rather than ``Extract(..., lookup_name='year')``.\n   297\t\n   298\tUsage example::\n   299\t\n   300\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   301\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Extract\n   302\t    >>> start = datetime(2015, 6, 15)\n   303\t    >>> end = datetime(2015, 7, 2)\n   304\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   305\t    ...    start_datetime=start, start_date=start.date(),\n   306\t    ...    end_datetime=end, end_date=end.date())\n   307\t    >>> # Add the experiment start year as a field in the QuerySet.\n   308\t    >>> experiment = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   309\t    ...    start_year=Extract('start_datetime', 'year')).get()\n   310\t    >>> experiment.start_year\n   311\t    2015\n   312\t    >>> # How many experiments completed in the same year in which they started?\n   313\t    >>> Experiment.objects.filter(\n   314\t    ...    start_datetime__year=Extract('end_datetime', 'year')).count()\n   315\t    1\n   316\t\n   317\t``DateField`` extracts\n   318\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   319\t\n   320\t.. class:: ExtractYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   321\t\n   322\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'year'\n   323\t\n   324\t.. class:: ExtractIsoYear(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   325\t\n   326\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week-numbering year.\n   327\t\n   328\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_year'\n   329\t\n   330\t.. class:: ExtractMonth(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   331\t\n   332\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'month'\n   333\t\n   334\t.. class:: ExtractDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   335\t\n   336\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'day'\n   337\t\n   338\t.. class:: ExtractWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   339\t\n   340\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week_day'\n   341\t\n   342\t.. class:: ExtractIsoWeekDay(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   343\t\n   344\t    Returns the ISO-8601 week day with day 1 being Monday and day 7 being\n   345\t    Sunday.\n   346\t\n   347\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'iso_week_day'\n   348\t\n   349\t.. class:: ExtractWeek(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   350\t\n   351\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'week'\n   352\t\n   353\t.. class:: ExtractQuarter(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   354\t\n   355\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'quarter'\n   356\t\n   357\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('date_field', lookup_name)``. Each\n   358\tclass is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateField`` and ``DateTimeField``\n   359\tas ``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__year``.\n   360\t\n   361\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Extract`` subclasses\n   362\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   363\t\n   364\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   365\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   366\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   367\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractMonth, ExtractQuarter, ExtractWeek,\n   368\t    ...     ExtractIsoWeekDay, ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   369\t    ... )\n   370\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   371\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   372\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   373\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   374\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   375\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   376\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_date'),\n   377\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_date'),\n   378\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_date'),\n   379\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_date'),\n   380\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_date'),\n   381\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_date'),\n   382\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_date'),\n   383\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_date'),\n   384\t    ... ).values(\n   385\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'quarter', 'month', 'week', 'day', 'weekday',\n   386\t    ...     'isoweekday',\n   387\t    ... ).get(end_date__year=ExtractYear('start_date'))\n   388\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   389\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1}\n   390\t\n   391\t``DateTimeField`` extracts\n   392\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   393\t\n   394\tIn addition to the following, all extracts for ``DateField`` listed above may\n   395\talso be used on ``DateTimeField``\\s .\n   396\t\n   397\t.. class:: ExtractHour(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   398\t\n   399\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'hour'\n   400\t\n   401\t.. class:: ExtractMinute(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   402\t\n   403\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'minute'\n   404\t\n   405\t.. class:: ExtractSecond(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   406\t\n   407\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'second'\n   408\t\n   409\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Extract('datetime_field', lookup_name)``.\n   410\tEach class is also a ``Transform`` registered on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   411\t``__(lookup_name)``, e.g. ``__minute``.\n   412\t\n   413\t``DateTimeField`` examples::\n   414\t\n   415\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   416\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   417\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   418\t    ...     ExtractDay, ExtractHour, ExtractMinute, ExtractMonth,\n   419\t    ...     ExtractQuarter, ExtractSecond, ExtractWeek, ExtractIsoWeekDay,\n   420\t    ...     ExtractWeekDay, ExtractIsoYear, ExtractYear,\n   421\t    ... )\n   422\t    >>> start_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 23, 30, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   423\t    >>> end_2015 = datetime(2015, 6, 16, 13, 11, 27, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   424\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(\n   425\t    ...    start_datetime=start_2015, start_date=start_2015.date(),\n   426\t    ...    end_datetime=end_2015, end_date=end_2015.date())\n   427\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   428\t    ...     year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   429\t    ...     isoyear=ExtractIsoYear('start_datetime'),\n   430\t    ...     quarter=ExtractQuarter('start_datetime'),\n   431\t    ...     month=ExtractMonth('start_datetime'),\n   432\t    ...     week=ExtractWeek('start_datetime'),\n   433\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   434\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   435\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   436\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   437\t    ...     minute=ExtractMinute('start_datetime'),\n   438\t    ...     second=ExtractSecond('start_datetime'),\n   439\t    ... ).values(\n   440\t    ...     'year', 'isoyear', 'month', 'week', 'day',\n   441\t    ...     'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour', 'minute', 'second',\n   442\t    ... ).get(end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'))\n   443\t    {'year': 2015, 'isoyear': 2015, 'quarter': 2, 'month': 6, 'week': 25,\n   444\t     'day': 15, 'weekday': 2, 'isoweekday': 1, 'hour': 23, 'minute': 30,\n   445\t     'second': 1}\n   446\t\n   447\tWhen :setting:`USE_TZ` is ``True`` then datetimes are stored in the database\n   448\tin UTC. If a different timezone is active in Django, the datetime is converted\n   449\tto that timezone before the value is extracted. The example below converts to\n   450\tthe Melbourne timezone (UTC +10:00), which changes the day, weekday, and hour\n   451\tvalues that are returned::\n   452\t\n   453\t    >>> import pytz\n   454\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')  # UTC+10:00\n   455\t    >>> with timezone.override(melb):\n   456\t    ...    Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   457\t    ...        day=ExtractDay('start_datetime'),\n   458\t    ...        weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   459\t    ...        isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime'),\n   460\t    ...        hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime'),\n   461\t    ...    ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   462\t    ...        end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   463\t    ...    )\n   464\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   465\t\n   466\tExplicitly passing the timezone to the ``Extract`` function behaves in the same\n   467\tway, and takes priority over an active timezone::\n   468\t\n   469\t    >>> import pytz\n   470\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   471\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   472\t    ...     day=ExtractDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   473\t    ...     weekday=ExtractWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   474\t    ...     isoweekday=ExtractIsoWeekDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   475\t    ...     hour=ExtractHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   476\t    ... ).values('day', 'weekday', 'isoweekday', 'hour').get(\n   477\t    ...     end_datetime__year=ExtractYear('start_datetime'),\n   478\t    ... )\n   479\t    {'day': 16, 'weekday': 3, 'isoweekday': 2, 'hour': 9}\n   480\t\n   481\t``Now``\n   482\t-------\n   483\t\n   484\t.. class:: Now()\n   485\t\n   486\tReturns the database server's current date and time when the query is executed,\n   487\ttypically using the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP``.\n   488\t\n   489\tUsage example::\n   490\t\n   491\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Now\n   492\t    >>> Article.objects.filter(published__lte=Now())\n   493\t    <QuerySet [<Article: How to Django>]>\n   494\t\n   495\t.. admonition:: PostgreSQL considerations\n   496\t\n   497\t    On PostgreSQL, the SQL ``CURRENT_TIMESTAMP`` returns the time that the\n   498\t    current transaction started. Therefore for cross-database compatibility,\n   499\t    ``Now()`` uses ``STATEMENT_TIMESTAMP`` instead. If you need the transaction\n   500\t    timestamp, use :class:`django.contrib.postgres.functions.TransactionNow`.\n   501\t\n   502\t``Trunc``\n   503\t---------\n   504\t\n   505\t.. class:: Trunc(expression, kind, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   506\t\n   507\tTruncates a date up to a significant component.\n   508\t\n   509\tWhen you only care if something happened in a particular year, hour, or day,\n   510\tbut not the exact second, then ``Trunc`` (and its subclasses) can be useful to\n   511\tfilter or aggregate your data. For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\n   512\tthe number of sales per day.\n   513\t\n   514\t``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\n   515\t``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\n   516\tpart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\n   517\t``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``. It returns a datetime, date, or time\n   518\tdepending on ``output_field``, with fields up to ``kind`` set to their minimum\n   519\tvalue. If ``output_field`` is omitted, it will default to the ``output_field``\n   520\tof ``expression``. A ``tzinfo`` subclass, usually provided by ``pytz``, can be\n   521\tpassed to truncate a value in a specific timezone.\n   522\t\n   523\tThe ``is_dst`` parameter indicates whether or not ``pytz`` should interpret\n   524\tnonexistent and ambiguous datetimes in daylight saving time. By default (when\n   525\t``is_dst=None``), ``pytz`` raises an exception for such datetimes.\n   526\t\n   527\tGiven the datetime ``2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321+00:00``, the built-in ``kind``\\s\n   528\treturn:\n   529\t\n   530\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   531\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   532\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+00:00\n   533\t* \"week\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   534\t* \"day\": 2015-06-15 00:00:00+00:00\n   535\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-15 14:00:00+00:00\n   536\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-15 14:30:00+00:00\n   537\t* \"second\": 2015-06-15 14:30:50+00:00\n   538\t\n   539\tIf a different timezone like ``Australia/Melbourne`` is active in Django, then\n   540\tthe datetime is converted to the new timezone before the value is truncated.\n   541\tThe timezone offset for Melbourne in the example date above is +10:00. The\n   542\tvalues returned when this timezone is active will be:\n   543\t\n   544\t* \"year\": 2015-01-01 00:00:00+11:00\n   545\t* \"quarter\": 2015-04-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   546\t* \"month\": 2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00\n   547\t* \"week\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   548\t* \"day\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   549\t* \"hour\": 2015-06-16 00:00:00+10:00\n   550\t* \"minute\": 2015-06-16 00:30:00+10:00\n   551\t* \"second\": 2015-06-16 00:30:50+10:00\n   552\t\n   553\tThe year has an offset of +11:00 because the result transitioned into daylight\n   554\tsaving time.\n   555\t\n   556\tEach ``kind`` above has a corresponding ``Trunc`` subclass (listed below) that\n   557\tshould typically be used instead of the more verbose equivalent,\n   558\te.g. use ``TruncYear(...)`` rather than ``Trunc(..., kind='year')``.\n   559\t\n   560\tThe subclasses are all defined as transforms, but they aren't registered with\n   561\tany fields, because the lookup names are already reserved by the ``Extract``\n   562\tsubclasses.\n   563\t\n   564\tUsage example::\n   565\t\n   566\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   567\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count, DateTimeField\n   568\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Trunc\n   569\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321))\n   570\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123))\n   571\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=datetime(2015, 12, 25, 10, 5, 27, 999))\n   572\t    >>> experiments_per_day = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   573\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   574\t    ... ).values('start_day').annotate(experiments=Count('id'))\n   575\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_day:\n   576\t    ...     print(exp['start_day'], exp['experiments'])\n   577\t    ...\n   578\t    2015-06-15 00:00:00 2\n   579\t    2015-12-25 00:00:00 1\n   580\t    >>> experiments = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   581\t    ...    start_day=Trunc('start_datetime', 'day', output_field=DateTimeField())\n   582\t    ... ).filter(start_day=datetime(2015, 6, 15))\n   583\t    >>> for exp in experiments:\n   584\t    ...     print(exp.start_datetime)\n   585\t    ...\n   586\t    2015-06-15 14:30:50.000321\n   587\t    2015-06-15 14:40:02.000123\n   588\t\n   589\t``DateField`` truncation\n   590\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   591\t\n   592\t.. class:: TruncYear(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   593\t\n   594\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'year'\n   595\t\n   596\t.. class:: TruncMonth(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   597\t\n   598\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'month'\n   599\t\n   600\t.. class:: TruncWeek(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   601\t\n   602\t    Truncates to midnight on the Monday of the week.\n   603\t\n   604\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'week'\n   605\t\n   606\t.. class:: TruncQuarter(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   607\t\n   608\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'quarter'\n   609\t\n   610\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('date_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   611\tall parts of the date up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering dates\n   612\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   613\t``DateField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   614\t\n   615\tSince ``DateField``\\s don't have a time component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   616\tthat deal with date-parts can be used with ``DateField``::\n   617\t\n   618\t    >>> from datetime import datetime\n   619\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   620\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import TruncMonth, TruncYear\n   621\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   622\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   623\t    >>> start2 = datetime(2015, 6, 15, 14, 40, 2, 123, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   624\t    >>> start3 = datetime(2015, 12, 31, 17, 5, 27, 999, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   625\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   626\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start2, start_date=start2.date())\n   627\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start3, start_date=start3.date())\n   628\t    >>> experiments_per_year = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   629\t    ...    year=TruncYear('start_date')).values('year').annotate(\n   630\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   631\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_year:\n   632\t    ...     print(exp['year'], exp['experiments'])\n   633\t    ...\n   634\t    2014-01-01 1\n   635\t    2015-01-01 2\n   636\t\n   637\t    >>> import pytz\n   638\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   639\t    >>> experiments_per_month = Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   640\t    ...    month=TruncMonth('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb)).values('month').annotate(\n   641\t    ...    experiments=Count('id'))\n   642\t    >>> for exp in experiments_per_month:\n   643\t    ...     print(exp['month'], exp['experiments'])\n   644\t    ...\n   645\t    2015-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   646\t    2016-01-01 00:00:00+11:00 1\n   647\t    2014-06-01 00:00:00+10:00 1\n   648\t\n   649\t``DateTimeField`` truncation\n   650\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   651\t\n   652\t.. class:: TruncDate(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   653\t\n   654\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'date'\n   655\t    .. attribute:: output_field = DateField()\n   656\t\n   657\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   658\t\n   659\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   660\t\n   661\t``TruncDate`` casts ``expression`` to a date rather than using the built-in SQL\n   662\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on  ``DateTimeField`` as\n   663\t``__date``.\n   664\t\n   665\t.. class:: TruncTime(expression, tzinfo=None, **extra)\n   666\t\n   667\t    .. attribute:: lookup_name = 'time'\n   668\t    .. attribute:: output_field = TimeField()\n   669\t\n   670\t    .. versionchanged:: 3.2\n   671\t\n   672\t        The ``tzinfo`` parameter was added.\n   673\t\n   674\t``TruncTime`` casts ``expression`` to a time rather than using the built-in SQL\n   675\ttruncate function. It's also registered as a transform on ``DateTimeField`` as\n   676\t``__time``.\n   677\t\n   678\t.. class:: TruncDay(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   679\t\n   680\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'day'\n   681\t\n   682\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   683\t\n   684\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   685\t\n   686\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   687\t\n   688\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   689\t\n   690\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   691\t\n   692\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   693\t\n   694\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('datetime_field', kind)``. They\n   695\ttruncate all parts of the date up to ``kind`` and allow grouping or filtering\n   696\tdatetimes with less precision. ``expression`` must have an ``output_field`` of\n   697\t``DateTimeField``.\n   698\t\n   699\tUsage example::\n   700\t\n   701\t    >>> from datetime import date, datetime\n   702\t    >>> from django.db.models import Count\n   703\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import (\n   704\t    ...     TruncDate, TruncDay, TruncHour, TruncMinute, TruncSecond,\n   705\t    ... )\n   706\t    >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   707\t    >>> import pytz\n   708\t    >>> start1 = datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, 321, tzinfo=timezone.utc)\n   709\t    >>> Experiment.objects.create(start_datetime=start1, start_date=start1.date())\n   710\t    >>> melb = pytz.timezone('Australia/Melbourne')\n   711\t    >>> Experiment.objects.annotate(\n   712\t    ...     date=TruncDate('start_datetime'),\n   713\t    ...     day=TruncDay('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   714\t    ...     hour=TruncHour('start_datetime', tzinfo=melb),\n   715\t    ...     minute=TruncMinute('start_datetime'),\n   716\t    ...     second=TruncSecond('start_datetime'),\n   717\t    ... ).values('date', 'day', 'hour', 'minute', 'second').get()\n   718\t    {'date': datetime.date(2014, 6, 15),\n   719\t     'day': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   720\t     'hour': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 16, 0, 0, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Australia/Melbourne' AEST+10:00:00 STD>),\n   721\t     'minute': 'minute': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, tzinfo=<UTC>),\n   722\t     'second': datetime.datetime(2014, 6, 15, 14, 30, 50, tzinfo=<UTC>)\n   723\t    }\n   724\t\n   725\t``TimeField`` truncation\n   726\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n   727\t\n   728\t.. class:: TruncHour(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   729\t    :noindex:\n   730\t\n   731\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'hour'\n   732\t\n   733\t.. class:: TruncMinute(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   734\t    :noindex:\n   735\t\n   736\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'minute'\n   737\t\n   738\t.. class:: TruncSecond(expression, output_field=None, tzinfo=None, is_dst=None, **extra)\n   739\t    :noindex:\n   740\t\n   741\t    .. attribute:: kind = 'second'\n   742\t\n   743\tThese are logically equivalent to ``Trunc('time_field', kind)``. They truncate\n   744\tall parts of the time up to ``kind`` which allows grouping or filtering times\n   745\twith less precision. ``expression`` can have an ``output_field`` of either\n   746\t``TimeField`` or ``DateTimeField``.\n   747\t\n   748\tSince ``TimeField``\\s don't have a date component, only ``Trunc`` subclasses\n   749\tthat deal with time-parts can be used with  print(exp['hour'], exp['experiments'])\n   777\t    ...\n   778\t    2014-06-16 00:00:00+10:00 2\n   779\t    2016-01-01 04:00:00+11:00 1\n   780\t\n   781\t.. _math-functions:\n   782\t\n   783\tMath Functions\n   784\t==============\n   785\t\n   786\tWe'll be using the following model in math function examples::\n   787\t\n   788\t    class Vector(models.Model):\n   789\t        x = models.FloatField()\n   790\t        y = models.FloatField()\n   791\t\n   792\t``Abs``\n   793\t-------\n   794\t\n   795\t.. class:: Abs(expression, **extra)\n   796\t\n   797\tReturns the absolute value of a numeric field or expression.\n   798\t\n   799\tUsage example::\n   800\t\n   801\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Abs\n   802\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=-0.5, y=1.1)\n   803\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_abs=Abs('x'), y_abs=Abs('y')).get()\n   804\t    >>> vector.x_abs, vector.y_abs\n   805\t    (0.5, 1.1)\n   806\t\n   807\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n   808\t\n   809\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n   810\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Abs\n   811\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Abs)\n   812\t    >>> # Get vectors inside the unit cube\n   813\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__abs__lt=1, y__abs__lt=1)\n   814\t\n   815\t``ACos``\n   816\t--------\n   817\t\n   818\t.. class:: ACos(expression, **extra)\n   819\t\n   820\tReturns the arccosine of a numeric field or expression. ForcedTimeZoneDatabaseTests(TransactionTestCase):\n   598\t    \"\"\"\n   599\t    Test the TIME_ZONE database configuration parameter.\n   600\t\n   601\t    Since this involves reading and writing PyYAML 5.3+ loads timezones\n   645\t    #   correctly.\n   646\t    # Tests are adapted to take these quirks into account.\n   647\t\n   648\t    def assert_python_contains_datetime(self, objects, dt):\n   649\t        self.assertEqual(objects[0]['fields']['dt'], dt)\n   650\t\n   651\t    def assert_json_contains_datetime(self, json, dt):\n   652\t        self.assertIn('\"fields\": {\"dt\": \"%s\"}' % dt, json)\n   653\t\n   654\t    def assert_xml_contains_datetime(self, xml, dt):\n   655\t        field = parseString(xml).getElementsByTagName('field')[0]\n   656\t        self.assertXMLEqual(field.childNodes[0].wholeText, dt)\n   657\t\n   658\t    def assert_yaml_contains_datetime(self, yaml, dt):\n   659\t        # Depending on the yaml dumper, '!timestamp' might be absent\n   660\t        self.assertRegex(yaml, r\"\\n  fields: {dt: !(!timestamp)? Set this to a string such as\n  3161\t``\"example.com\"`` for cross-domain cookies, or use ``None`` for a standard\n  3162\tdomain cookie.\n  3163\t\n  3164\tTo use cross-domain cookies with :setting:`CSRF_USE_SESSIONS`, you must include\n  3165\ta leading dot (e.g. ``\".example.com\"``) to accommodate the CSRF middleware's\n  3166\treferer checking.\n  3167\t\n  3168\tBe cautious when updating this setting on a production site. If you update\n  3169\tthis setting to enable cross-domain cookies on a site that previously used\n  3170\tstandard domain cookies, existing user cookies will be set to the old\n  3171\tdomain. This may result in them being unable to log in as long as these cookies\n  3172\tpersist.\n  3173\t\n  3174\tThis setting also affects cookies set by :mod:`django.contrib.messages`.\n  3175\t\n  3176\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY\n  3177\t\n  3178\t``SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY``\n  3179\t---------------------------\n  3180\t\n  3181\tDefault: ``True``\n  3182\t\n  3183\tWhether to use ``HttpOnly`` flag on the session cookie. If this is set to\n  3184\t``True``, client-side JavaScript will not be able to access the session\n  3185\tcookie.\n  3186\t\n  3187\tHttpOnly_ is a flag included in a Set-Cookie HTTP response header. It's part of\n  3188\tthe :rfc:`6265#section-4.1.2.6` standard for cookies and can be a useful way to\n  3189\tmitigate the risk of a client-side script accessing the protected cookie data.\n  3190\t\n  3191\tThis makes it less trivial for an attacker to escalate a cross-site scripting\n  3192\tvulnerability into full hijacking of a user's session. There aren't many good\n  3193\treasons for turning this off. Your code shouldn't read session cookies from\n  3194\tJavaScript.\n  3195\t\n  3196\t.. _HttpOnly: https://owasp.org/www-community/HttpOnly\n  3197\t\n  3198\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_NAME\n  3199\t\n  3200\t``SESSION_COOKIE_NAME``\n  3201\t-----------------------\n  3202\t\n  3203\tDefault: ``'sessionid'``\n  3204\t\n  3205\tThe name of the cookie to use for sessions. This can be whatever you want\n  3206\t(as long as it's different from the other cookie names in your application).\n  3207\t\n  3208\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_PATH\n  3209\t\n  3210\t``SESSION_COOKIE_PATH``\n  3211\t-----------------------\n  3212\t\n  3213\tDefault: ``'/'``\n  3214\t\n  3215\tThe path set on the session cookie. This should either match the URL path of your\n  3216\tDjango installation or be parent of that path.\n  3217\t\n  3218\tThis is useful if you have multiple Django instances running under the same\n  3219\thostname. They can use different cookie paths, and each instance will only see\n  3220\tits own session cookie.\n  3221\t\n  3222\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE\n  3223\t\n  3224\t``SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE``\n  3225\t---------------------------\n  3226\t\n  3227\tDefault: ``'Lax'``\n  3228\t\n  3229\tThe value of the `SameSite`_ flag on the session cookie. This flag prevents the\n  3230\tcookie from being sent in cross-site requests thus preventing CSRF attacks and\n  3231\tmaking some methods of stealing session cookie impossible.\n  3232\t\n  3233\tPossible values for the setting are:\n  3234\t\n  3235\t* ``'Strict'``: prevents the cookie from being sent by the browser to the\n  3236\t  target site in all cross-site browsing context, even when following a regular\n  3237\t  link.\n  3238\t\n  3239\t  For example, for a GitHub-like website this would mean that if a logged-in\n  3240\t  user follows a link to a private GitHub project posted on a corporate\n  3241\t  discussion forum or email, GitHub will not receive the session cookie and the\n  3242\t  user won't be able to access the project. A bank website, however, most\n  3243\t  likely doesn't want to allow any transactional pages to be linked from\n  3244\t  external sites so the ``'Strict'`` flag would be appropriate.\n  3245\t\n  3246\t* ``'Lax'`` (default): provides a balance between security and usability for\n  3247\t  websites that want to maintain user's logged-in session after the user\n  3248\t  arrives from an external link.\n  3249\t\n  3250\t  In the GitHub scenario, the session cookie would be allowed when following a\n  3251\t  regular link from an external website and be blocked in CSRF-prone request\n  3252\t  methods (e.g. ``POST``).\n  3253\t\n  3254\t* ``'None'`` (string): the session cookie will be sent with all same-site and\n  3255\t  cross-site requests.\n  3256\t\n  3257\t* ``False``: disables the flag.\n  3258\t\n  3259\t.. note::\n  3260\t\n  3261\t    Modern browsers provide a more secure default policy for the ``SameSite``\n  3262\t    flag and will assume ``Lax`` for cookies without an explicit value set.\n  3263\t\n  3264\t.. _SameSite: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie/SameSite\n  3265\t\n  3266\t.. setting:: SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE\n  3267\t\n  3268\t``SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE``\n  3269\t-------------------------\n  3270\t\n  3271\tDefault: ``False``\n  3272\t\n  3273\tWhether to use a secure cookie for the session cookie. If this is set to\n  3274\t``True``, the cookie will be marked as \"secure\", which means browsers may\n  3275\tensure that the cookie is only sent under an HTTPS connection.\n  3276\t\n  3277\tLeaving this setting off isn't a good idea because an attacker could capture an\n  3278\tunencrypted session cookie with a packet sniffer and use the cookie to hijack\n  3279\tthe user's session.\n  3280\t\n  3281\t.. setting:: SESSION_ENGINE\n  3282\t\n  3283\t``SESSION_ENGINE``\n  3284\t------------------\n  3285\t\n  3286\tDefault: ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.db'``\n  3287\t\n  3288\tControls where Django stores session data. Included engines are:\n  3289\t\n  3290\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.db'``\n  3291\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.file'``\n  3292\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.cache'``\n  3293\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.cached_db'``\n  3294\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.backends.signed_cookies'``\n  3295\t\n  3296\tSee :ref:`configuring-sessions` for more details.\n  3297\t\n  3298\t.. setting:: SESSION_EXPIRE_AT_BROWSER_CLOSE\n  3299\t\n  3300\t``SESSION_EXPIRE_AT_BROWSER_CLOSE``\n  3301\t-----------------------------------\n  3302\t\n  3303\tDefault: ``False``\n  3304\t\n  3305\tWhether to expire the session when the user closes their browser. See\n  3306\t:ref:`browser-length-vs-persistent-sessions`.\n  3307\t\n  3308\t.. setting:: SESSION_FILE_PATH\n  3309\t\n  3310\t``SESSION_FILE_PATH``\n  3311\t---------------------\n  3312\t\n  3313\tDefault: ``None``\n  3314\t\n  3315\tIf you're using file-based session storage, this sets the directory in\n  3316\twhich Django will store session data. When the default value (``None``) is\n  3317\tused, Django will use the standard temporary directory for the system.\n  3318\t\n  3319\t\n  3320\t.. setting:: SESSION_SAVE_EVERY_REQUEST\n  3321\t\n  3322\t``SESSION_SAVE_EVERY_REQUEST``\n  3323\t------------------------------\n  3324\t\n  3325\tDefault: ``False``\n  3326\t\n  3327\tWhether to save the session data on every request. If this is ``False``\n  3328\t(default), then the session data will only be saved if it has been modified --\n  3329\tthat is, if any of its dictionary values have been assigned or deleted. Empty\n  3330\tsessions won't be created, even if this setting is active.\n  3331\t\n  3332\t.. setting:: SESSION_SERIALIZER\n  3333\t\n  3334\t``SESSION_SERIALIZER``\n  3335\t----------------------\n  3336\t\n  3337\tDefault: ``'django.contrib.sessions.serializers.JSONSerializer'``\n  3338\t\n  3339\tFull import path of a serializer class to use for serializing session data.\n  3340\tIncluded serializers are:\n  3341\t\n  3342\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.serializers.PickleSerializer'``\n  3343\t* ``'django.contrib.sessions.serializers.JSONSerializer'``\n  3344\t\n  3345\tSee :ref:`session_serialization` for details, including a warning regarding\n  3346\tpossible remote code execution when using\n  3347\t:class:`~django.contrib.sessions.serializers.PickleSerializer`.\n  3348\t\n  3349\tSites\n  3350\t=====\n  3351\t\n  3352\tSettings for :mod:`django.contrib.sites`.\n  3353\t\n  3354\t.. setting:: SITE_ID\n  3355\t\n  3356\t``SITE_ID``\n  3357\t-----------\n  3358\t\n  3359\tDefault: Not defined\n  3360\t\n  3361\tThe ID, as an integer, of the current site in the ``django_site`` database\n  3362\ttable. This is used so that application data can hook into specific sites\n  3363\tand a single database can manage content for multiple sites.\n  3364\t\n  3365\t\n  3366\t.. _settings-staticfiles:\n  3367\t\n  3368\tStatic Files\n  3369\t============\n  3370\t\n  3371\tSettings for :mod:`django.contrib.staticfiles`.\n  3372\t\n  3373\t.. setting:: STATIC_ROOT\n  3374\t\n  3375\t``STATIC_ROOT``\n  3376\t---------------\n  3377\t\n  3378\tDefault: ``None``\n  3379\t\n  3380\tThe absolute path to the directory where :djadmin:`collectstatic` will collect\n  3381\tstatic files for deployment.\n  3382\t\n  3383\tExample: ``\"/var/www/example.com/static/\"``\n  3384\t\n  3385\tIf the :doc:`staticfiles</ref/contrib/staticfiles>` contrib app is enabled\n  3386\t(as in the default project template), the :djadmin:`collectstatic` management\n  3387\tcommand will collect static files into this directory. See the how-to on\n  3388\t:doc:`managing static files</howto/static-files/index>` for more details about\n  3389\tusage.\n  3390\t\n  3391\t.. warning::\n  3392\t\n  3393\t    This should be an initially empty destination directory for collecting\n  3394\t    your static files from their permanent locations into one directory for\n  3395\t    ease of deployment; it is **not** a place to store your static files\n  3396\t    permanently. You should do that in directories that will be found by\n  3397\t    :doc:`staticfiles</ref/contrib/staticfiles>`’s\n  3398\t    :setting:`finders<STATICFILES_FINDERS>`, which by default, are\n  3399\t    ``'static/'`` app sub-directories and any directories you include in\n  3400\t    :setting:`STATICFILES_DIRS`).\n  3401\t\n  3402\t.. setting:: STATIC_URL\n  3403\t\n  3404\t``STATIC_URL``\n  3405\t--------------\n  3406\t\n  3407\tDefault: ``None``\n  3408\t\n  3409\tURL to use when referring to static files located in :setting:`STATIC_ROOT`.\n  3410\t\n  3411\tExample: ``\"static/\"`` or ``\"http://static.example.com/\"``\n  3412\t\n  3413\tIf not ``None``, this will be used as the base path for\n  3414\t:ref:`asset definitions<form-asset-paths>` (the ``Media`` class) and the\n  3415\t:doc:`staticfiles app</ref/contrib/staticfiles>`.\n  3416\t\n  3417\tIt must end in a slash if set to a non-empty value.\n  3418\t\n  3419\tYou may need to :ref:`configure these files to be served in development\n  3420\t<serving-static-files-in-development>` and will definitely need to do so\n  3421\t:doc:`in production </howto/static-files/deployment>`.\n  3422\t\n  3423\t.. note::\n  3424\t\n  3425\t    If :setting:`STATIC_URL` is a relative path, then it will be prefixed by\n  3426\t    the server-provided value of ``SCRIPT_NAME`` (or ``/`` if not set). This\n  3427\t    makes it easier to serve a Django application in a subpath without adding\n  3428\t    an extra configuration to the settings.\n  3429\t\n  3430\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_DIRS\n  3431\t\n  3432\t``STATICFILES_DIRS``\n  3433\t--------------------\n  3434\t\n  3435\tDefault: ``[]`` (Empty list)\n  3436\t\n  3437\tThis setting defines the additional locations the staticfiles app will traverse\n  3438\tif the ``FileSystemFinder`` finder is enabled, e.g. if you use the\n  3439\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` or :djadmin:`findstatic` management command or use the\n  3440\tstatic file serving view.\n  3441\t\n  3442\tThis should be set to a list of strings that contain full paths to\n  3443\tyour additional files directory(ies) e.g.::\n  3444\t\n  3445\t    STATICFILES_DIRS = [\n  3446\t        \"/home/special.polls.com/polls/static\",\n  3447\t        \"/home/polls.com/polls/static\",\n  3448\t        \"/opt/webfiles/common\",\n  3449\t    ]\n  3450\t\n  3451\tNote that these paths should use Unix-style forward slashes, even on Windows\n  3452\t(e.g. ``\"C:/Users/user/mysite/extra_static_content\"``).\n  3453\t\n  3454\t.. _staticfiles-dirs-prefixes:\n  3455\t\n  3456\tPrefixes (optional)\n  3457\t~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~\n  3458\t\n  3459\tIn case you want to refer to files in one of the locations with an additional\n  3460\tnamespace, you can **optionally** provide a prefix as ``(prefix, path)``\n  3461\ttuples, e.g.::\n  3462\t\n  3463\t    STATICFILES_DIRS = [\n  3464\t        # ...\n  3465\t        (\"downloads\", \"/opt/webfiles/stats\"),\n  3466\t    ]\n  3467\t\n  3468\tFor example, assuming you have :setting:`STATIC_URL` set to ``'static/'``, the\n  3469\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` management command would collect the \"stats\" files\n  3470\tin a ``'downloads'`` subdirectory of :setting:`STATIC_ROOT`.\n  3471\t\n  3472\tThis would allow you to refer to the local file\n  3473\t``'/opt/webfiles/stats/polls_20101022.tar.gz'`` with\n  3474\t``'/static/downloads/polls_20101022.tar.gz'`` in your templates, e.g.:\n  3475\t\n  3476\t.. code-block:: html+django\n  3477\t\n  3478\t    <a href=\"{% static 'downloads/polls_20101022.tar.gz' %}\">\n  3479\t\n  3480\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_STORAGE\n  3481\t\n  3482\t``STATICFILES_STORAGE``\n  3483\t-----------------------\n  3484\t\n  3485\tDefault: ``'django.contrib.staticfiles.storage.StaticFilesStorage'``\n  3486\t\n  3487\tThe file storage engine to use when collecting static files with the\n  3488\t:djadmin:`collectstatic` management command.\n  3489\t\n  3490\tA ready-to-use instance of the storage backend defined in this setting\n  3491\tcan be found at ``django.contrib.staticfiles.storage.staticfiles_storage``.\n  3492\t\n  3493\tFor an example, see :ref:`staticfiles-from-cdn`.\n  3494\t\n  3495\t.. setting:: STATICFILES_FINDERS\n  3496\t\n  3497\t``STATICFILES_FINDERS``\n  3498\t-----------------------\n  3499\t\n  3500\tDefault::\n  3501\t\n  3502\t    [\n  3503\t        'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder',\n  3504\t        'django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder',\n  3505\t    ]\n  3506\t\n  3507\tThe list of finder backends that know how to find static files in\n  3508\tvarious locations.\n  3509\t\n  3510\tThe default will find files stored in the :setting:`STATICFILES_DIRS` setting\n  3511\t(using ``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.FileSystemFinder``) and in a\n  3512\t``static`` subdirectory of each app (using\n  3513\t``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.AppDirectoriesFinder``). If multiple\n  3514\tfiles with the same name are present, the first file that is found will be\n  3515\tused.\n  3516\t\n  3517\tOne finder is disabled by default:\n  3518\t``django.contrib.staticfiles.finders.DefaultStorageFinder``. If added to\n  3519\tyour :setting:`STATICFILES_FINDERS` setting, it will look for static files in\n  3520\tthe default file storage as defined by the :setting:`DEFAULT_FILE_STORAGE`\n  3521\tsetting.\n  3522\t\n  3523\t.. note::\n  3524\t\n  3525\t    When using the ``AppDirectoriesFinder`` finder, make sure your apps\n  3526\t    can be found by staticfiles by adding the app to the\n  3527\t    :setting:`INSTALLED_APPS` setting of your site.\n  3528\t\n  3529\tStatic file finders are currently considered a private interface, and this\n  3530\tinterface is thus undocumented.\n  3531\t\n  3532\tCore Settings Topical Index\n  3533\t===========================\n  3534\t\n  3535\tCache\n  3536\t-----\n  3537\t* :setting:`CACHES`\n  3538\t* Whether half\n  1159\tvalues are rounded up or down depends on the database.\n  1160\t\n  1161\tUsage example::\n  1162\t\n  1163\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Round\n  1164\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.37)\n  1165\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_r=Round('x'), y_r=Round('y', precision=1)).get()\n  1166\t    >>> vector.x_r, vector.y_r\n  1167\t    (5.0, -2.4)\n  1168\t\n  1169\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1170\t\n  1171\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1172\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Round\n  1173\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Round)\n  1174\t    >>> # Get vectors whose round() is less than 20\n  1175\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__round__lt=20, y__round__lt=20)\n  1176\t\n  1177\t.. versionchanged:: 4.0\n  1178\t\n  1179\t    The ``precision`` argument was added.\n  1180\t\n  1181\t``Sign``\n  1182\t--------\n  1183\t\n  1184\t.. class:: Sign(expression, **extra)\n  1185\t\n  1186\tReturns the sign (-1, 0, 1) of a numeric field or expression.\n  1187\t\n  1188\tUsage example::\n  1189\t\n  1190\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sign\n  1191\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.3)\n  1192\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sign=Sign('x'), y_sign=Sign('y')).get()\n  1193\t    >>> vector.x_sign, vector.y_sign\n  1194\t    (1, -1)\n  1195\t\n  1196\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1197\t\n  1198\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1199\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sign\n  1200\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sign)\n  1201\t    >>> # Get vectors whose signs of components are less than 0.\n  1202\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sign__lt=0, y__sign__lt=0)\n  1203\t\n  1204\t``Sin``\n  1205\t-------\n  1206\t\n  1207\t.. class:: Sin(expression, **extra)\n  1208\t\n  1209\tReturns the sine of a numeric field or expression.\n  1210\t\n  1211\tUsage example::\n  1212\t\n  1213\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sin\n  1214\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=5.4, y=-2.3)\n  1215\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sin=Sin('x'), y_sin=Sin('y')).get()\n  1216\t    >>> vector.x_sin, vector.y_sin\n  1217\t    (-0.7727644875559871, -0.7457052121767203)\n  1218\t\n  1219\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1220\t\n  1221\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1222\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sin\n  1223\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sin)\n  1224\t    >>> # Get vectors whose sin() is less than 0\n  1225\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sin__lt=0, y__sin__lt=0)\n  1226\t\n  1227\t``Sqrt``\n  1228\t--------\n  1229\t\n  1230\t.. class:: Sqrt(expression, **extra)\n  1231\t\n  1232\tReturns the square root of a nonnegative numeric field or expression.\n  1233\t\n  1234\tUsage example::\n  1235\t\n  1236\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sqrt\n  1237\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=4.0, y=12.0)\n  1238\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_sqrt=Sqrt('x'), y_sqrt=Sqrt('y')).get()\n  1239\t    >>> vector.x_sqrt, vector.y_sqrt\n  1240\t    (2.0, 3.46410)\n  1241\t\n  1242\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1243\t\n  1244\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1245\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Sqrt\n  1246\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Sqrt)\n  1247\t    >>> # Get vectors whose sqrt() is less than 5\n  1248\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__sqrt__lt=5, y__sqrt__lt=5)\n  1249\t\n  1250\t``Tan``\n  1251\t-------\n  1252\t\n  1253\t.. class:: Tan(expression, **extra)\n  1254\t\n  1255\tReturns the tangent of a numeric field or expression.\n  1256\t\n  1257\tUsage example::\n  1258\t\n  1259\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Tan\n  1260\t    >>> Vector.objects.create(x=0, y=12)\n  1261\t    >>> vector = Vector.objects.annotate(x_tan=Tan('x'), y_tan=Tan('y')).get()\n  1262\t    >>> vector.x_tan, vector.y_tan\n  1263\t    (0.0, -0.6358599286615808)\n  1264\t\n  1265\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1266\t\n  1267\t    >>> from django.db.models import FloatField\n  1268\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Tan\n  1269\t    >>> FloatField.register_lookup(Tan)\n  1270\t    >>> # Get vectors whose tangent is less than 0\n  1271\t    >>> vectors = Vector.objects.filter(x__tan__lt=0, y__tan__lt=0)\n  1272\t\n  1273\t.. _text-functions:\n  1274\t\n  1275\tText functions\n  1276\t==============\n  1277\t\n  1278\t``Chr``\n  1279\t-------\n  1280\t\n  1281\t.. class:: Chr(expression, **extra)\n  1282\t\n  1283\tAccepts a numeric field or expression and returns the text representation of\n  1284\tthe expression as a single character. It works the same as Python's :func:`chr`\n  1285\tfunction.\n  1286\t\n  1287\tLike :class:`Length`, it can be registered as a transform on ``IntegerField``.\n  1288\tThe default lookup name is ``chr``.\n  1289\t\n  1290\tUsage example::\n  1291\t\n  1292\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Chr\n  1293\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1294\t    >>> author = Author.objects.filter(name__startswith=Chr(ord('M'))).get()\n  1295\t    >>> print(author.name)\n  1296\t    Margaret Smith\n  1297\t\n  1298\t``Concat``\n  1299\t----------\n  1300\t\n  1301\t.. class:: Concat(*expressions, **extra)\n  1302\t\n  1303\tAccepts a list of at least two text fields or expressions and returns the\n  1304\tconcatenated text. Each argument must be of a text or char type. If you want\n  1305\tto concatenate a ``TextField()`` with a ``CharField()``, then be sure to tell\n  1306\tDjango that the ``output_field`` should be a ``TextField()``. Specifying an\n  1307\t``output_field`` is also required when concatenating a ``Value`` as in the\n  1308\texample below.\n  1309\t\n  1310\tThis function will never have a null result. On backends where a null argument\n  1311\tresults in the entire expression being null, Django will ensure that each null\n  1312\tpart is converted to an empty string first.\n  1313\t\n  1314\tUsage example::\n  1315\t\n  1316\t    >>> # Get the display name as \"name (goes_by)\"\n  1317\t    >>> from django.db.models import CharField, Value as V\n  1318\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Concat\n  1319\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith', goes_by='Maggie')\n  1320\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1321\t    ...     screen_name=Concat(\n  1322\t    ...         'name', V(' ('), 'goes_by', V(')'),\n  1323\t    ...         output_field=CharField()\n  1324\t    ...     )\n  1325\t    ... ).get()\n  1326\t    >>> print(author.screen_name)\n  1327\t    Margaret Smith (Maggie)\n  1328\t\n  1329\t``Left``\n  1330\t--------\n  1331\t\n  1332\t.. class:: Left(expression, length, **extra)\n  1333\t\n  1334\tReturns the first ``length`` characters of the given text field or expression.\n  1335\t\n  1336\tUsage example::\n  1337\t\n  1338\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Left\n  1339\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1340\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(first_initial=Left('name', 1)).get()\n  1341\t    >>> print(author.first_initial)\n  1342\t    M\n  1343\t\n  1344\t``Length``\n  1345\t----------\n  1346\t\n  1347\t.. class:: Length(expression, **extra)\n  1348\t\n  1349\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the number of characters\n  1350\tthe value has. If the expression is null, then the length will also be null.\n  1351\t\n  1352\tUsage example::\n  1353\t\n  1354\t    >>> # Get the length of the name and goes_by fields\n  1355\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Length\n  1356\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1357\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1358\t    ...    name_length=Length('name'),\n  1359\t    ...    goes_by_length=Length('goes_by')).get()\n  1360\t    >>> print(author.name_length, author.goes_by_length)\n  1361\t    (14, None)\n  1362\t\n  1363\tIt can also be registered as a transform. For example::\n  1364\t\n  1365\t    >>> from django.db.models import CharField\n  1366\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Length\n  1367\t    >>> CharField.register_lookup(Length)\n  1368\t    >>> # Get authors whose name is longer than 7 characters\n  1369\t    >>> authors = Author.objects.filter(name__length__gt=7)\n  1370\t\n  1371\t``Lower``\n  1372\t---------\n  1373\t\n  1374\t.. class:: Lower(expression, **extra)\n  1375\t\n  1376\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the lowercase\n  1377\trepresentation.\n  1378\t\n  1379\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1380\t\n  1381\tUsage example::\n  1382\t\n  1383\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Lower\n  1384\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1385\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_lower=Lower('name')).get()\n  1386\t    >>> print(author.name_lower)\n  1387\t    margaret smith\n  1388\t\n  1389\t``LPad``\n  1390\t--------\n  1391\t\n  1392\t.. class:: LPad(expression, length, fill_text=Value(' '), **extra)\n  1393\t\n  1394\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression padded on the left side\n  1395\twith ``fill_text`` so that the resulting value is ``length`` characters long.\n  1396\tThe default ``fill_text`` is a space.\n  1397\t\n  1398\tUsage example::\n  1399\t\n  1400\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value\n  1401\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import LPad\n  1402\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='John', alias='j')\n  1403\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=LPad('name', 8, Value('abc')))\n  1404\t    1\n  1405\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1406\t    abcaJohn\n  1407\t\n  1408\t``LTrim``\n  1409\t---------\n  1410\t\n  1411\t.. class:: LTrim(expression, **extra)\n  1412\t\n  1413\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.Trim`, but removes only leading\n  1414\tspaces.\n  1415\t\n  1416\t``MD5``\n  1417\t-------\n  1418\t\n  1419\t.. class:: MD5(expression, **extra)\n  1420\t\n  1421\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the MD5 hash of the\n  1422\tstring.\n  1423\t\n  1424\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1425\t\n  1426\tUsage example::\n  1427\t\n  1428\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import MD5\n  1429\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1430\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_md5=MD5('name')).get()\n  1431\t    >>> print(author.name_md5)\n  1432\t    749fb689816b2db85f5b169c2055b247\n  1433\t\n  1434\t``Ord``\n  1435\t-------\n  1436\t\n  1437\t.. class:: Ord(expression, **extra)\n  1438\t\n  1439\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the Unicode code point\n  1440\tvalue for the first character of that expression. It works similar to Python's\n  1441\t:func:`ord` function, but an exception isn't raised if the expression is more\n  1442\tthan one character long.\n  1443\t\n  1444\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1445\tThe default lookup name is ``ord``.\n  1446\t\n  1447\tUsage example::\n  1448\t\n  1449\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Ord\n  1450\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1451\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_code_point=Ord('name')).get()\n  1452\t    >>> print(author.name_code_point)\n  1453\t    77\n  1454\t\n  1455\t``Repeat``\n  1456\t----------\n  1457\t\n  1458\t.. class:: Repeat(expression, number, **extra)\n  1459\t\n  1460\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression repeated ``number``\n  1461\ttimes.\n  1462\t\n  1463\tUsage example::\n  1464\t\n  1465\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Repeat\n  1466\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='John', alias='j')\n  1467\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Repeat('name', 3))\n  1468\t    1\n  1469\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1470\t    JohnJohnJohn\n  1471\t\n  1472\t``Replace``\n  1473\t-----------\n  1474\t\n  1475\t.. class:: Replace(expression, text, replacement=Value(''), **extra)\n  1476\t\n  1477\tReplaces all occurrences of ``text`` with ``replacement`` in ``expression``.\n  1478\tThe default replacement text is the empty string. The arguments to the function\n  1479\tare case-sensitive.\n  1480\t\n  1481\tUsage example::\n  1482\t\n  1483\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value\n  1484\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Replace\n  1485\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Johnson')\n  1486\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1487\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Replace('name', Value('Margaret'), Value('Margareth')))\n  1488\t    2\n  1489\t    >>> Author.objects.values('name')\n  1490\t    <QuerySet [{'name': 'Margareth Johnson'}, {'name': 'Margareth Smith'}]>\n  1491\t\n  1492\t``Reverse``\n  1493\t-----------\n  1494\t\n  1495\t.. class:: Reverse(expression, **extra)\n  1496\t\n  1497\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the characters of that\n  1498\texpression in reverse order.\n  1499\t\n  1500\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`. The\n  1501\tdefault lookup name is ``reverse``.\n  1502\t\n  1503\tUsage example::\n  1504\t\n  1505\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Reverse\n  1506\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1507\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(backward=Reverse('name')).get()\n  1508\t    >>> print(author.backward)\n  1509\t    htimS teragraM\n  1510\t\n  1511\t``Right``\n  1512\t---------\n  1513\t\n  1514\t.. class:: Right(expression, length, **extra)\n  1515\t\n  1516\tReturns the last ``length`` characters of the given text field or expression.\n  1517\t\n  1518\tUsage example::\n  1519\t\n  1520\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Right\n  1521\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1522\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(last_letter=Right('name', 1)).get()\n  1523\t    >>> print(author.last_letter)\n  1524\t    h\n  1525\t\n  1526\t``RPad``\n  1527\t--------\n  1528\t\n  1529\t.. class:: RPad(expression, length, fill_text=Value(' '), **extra)\n  1530\t\n  1531\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.LPad`, but pads on the right\n  1532\tside.\n  1533\t\n  1534\t``RTrim``\n  1535\t---------\n  1536\t\n  1537\t.. class:: RTrim(expression, **extra)\n  1538\t\n  1539\tSimilar to :class:`~django.db.models.functions.Trim`, but removes only trailing\n  1540\tspaces.\n  1541\t\n  1542\t``SHA1``, ``SHA224``, ``SHA256``, ``SHA384``, and ``SHA512``\n  1543\t------------------------------------------------------------\n  1544\t\n  1545\t.. class:: SHA1(expression, **extra)\n  1546\t.. class:: SHA224(expression, **extra)\n  1547\t.. class:: SHA256(expression, **extra)\n  1548\t.. class:: SHA384(expression, **extra)\n  1549\t.. class:: SHA512(expression, **extra)\n  1550\t\n  1551\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the particular hash of\n  1552\tthe string.\n  1553\t\n  1554\tThey can also be registered as transforms as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1555\t\n  1556\tUsage example::\n  1557\t\n  1558\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import SHA1\n  1559\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1560\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_sha1=SHA1('name')).get()\n  1561\t    >>> print(author.name_sha1)\n  1562\t    b87efd8a6c991c390be5a68e8a7945a7851c7e5c\n  1563\t\n  1564\t.. admonition:: PostgreSQL\n  1565\t\n  1566\t    The `pgcrypto extension <https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/\n  1567\t    pgcrypto.html>`_ must be installed. You can use the\n  1568\t    :class:`~django.contrib.postgres.operations.CryptoExtension` migration\n  1569\t    operation to install it.\n  1570\t\n  1571\t.. admonition:: Oracle\n  1572\t\n  1573\t    Oracle doesn't support the ``SHA224`` function.\n  1574\t\n  1575\t``StrIndex``\n  1576\t------------\n  1577\t\n  1578\t.. class:: StrIndex(string, substring, **extra)\n  1579\t\n  1580\tReturns a positive integer corresponding to the 1-indexed position of the first\n  1581\toccurrence of ``substring`` inside ``string``, or 0 if ``substring`` is not\n  1582\tfound.\n  1583\t\n  1584\tUsage example::\n  1585\t\n  1586\t    >>> from django.db.models import Value as V\n  1587\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import StrIndex\n  1588\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1589\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Smith, Margaret')\n  1590\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Jackson')\n  1591\t    >>> Author.objects.filter(name='Margaret Jackson').annotate(\n  1592\t    ...     smith_index=StrIndex('name', V('Smith'))\n  1593\t    ... ).get().smith_index\n  1594\t    0\n  1595\t    >>> authors = Author.objects.annotate(\n  1596\t    ...    smith_index=StrIndex('name', V('Smith'))\n  1597\t    ... ).filter(smith_index__gt=0)\n  1598\t    <QuerySet [<Author: Margaret Smith>, <Author: Smith, Margaret>]>\n  1599\t\n  1600\t.. warning::\n  1601\t\n  1602\t    In MySQL, a database table's :ref:`collation<mysql-collation>` determines\n  1603\t    whether string comparisons (such as the ``expression`` and ``substring`` of\n  1604\t    this function) are case-sensitive. Comparisons are case-insensitive by\n  1605\t    default.\n  1606\t\n  1607\t``Substr``\n  1608\t----------\n  1609\t\n  1610\t.. class:: Substr(expression, pos, length=None, **extra)\n  1611\t\n  1612\tReturns a substring of length ``length`` from the field or expression starting\n  1613\tat position ``pos``. The position is 1-indexed, so the position must be greater\n  1614\tthan 0. If ``length`` is ``None``, then the rest of the string will be returned.\n  1615\t\n  1616\tUsage example::\n  1617\t\n  1618\t    >>> # Set the alias to the first 5 characters of the name as lowercase\n  1619\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Lower, Substr\n  1620\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1621\t    >>> Author.objects.update(alias=Lower(Substr('name', 1, 5)))\n  1622\t    1\n  1623\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(name='Margaret Smith').alias)\n  1624\t    marga\n  1625\t\n  1626\t``Trim``\n  1627\t--------\n  1628\t\n  1629\t.. class:: Trim(expression, **extra)\n  1630\t\n  1631\tReturns the value of the given text field or expression with leading and\n  1632\ttrailing spaces removed.\n  1633\t\n  1634\tUsage example::\n  1635\t\n  1636\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Trim\n  1637\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='  John  ', alias='j')\n  1638\t    >>> Author.objects.update(name=Trim('name'))\n  1639\t    1\n  1640\t    >>> print(Author.objects.get(alias='j').name)\n  1641\t    John\n  1642\t\n  1643\t``Upper``\n  1644\t---------\n  1645\t\n  1646\t.. class:: Upper(expression, **extra)\n  1647\t\n  1648\tAccepts a single text field or expression and returns the uppercase\n  1649\trepresentation.\n  1650\t\n  1651\tIt can also be registered as a transform as described in :class:`Length`.\n  1652\t\n  1653\tUsage example::\n  1654\t\n  1655\t    >>> from django.db.models.functions import Upper\n  1656\t    >>> Author.objects.create(name='Margaret Smith')\n  1657\t    >>> author = Author.objects.annotate(name_upper=Upper('name')).get()\n  1658\t    >>> print(author.name_upper)\n  1659\t    MARGARET SMITH\n  1660\t\n  1661\t.. _window-functions:\n  1662\t\n  1663\tWindow functions\n  1664\t================\n  1665\t\n  1666\tThere are a number of functions to use in a\n  1667\t:class:`~django.db.models.expressions.Window` expression for computing the rank\n  1668\tof elements or the :class:`Ntile` of some rows.\n  1669\t\n  1670\t``CumeDist``\n  1671\t------------\n  1672\t\n  1673\t.. class:: CumeDist(*expressions, **extra)\n  1674\t\n  1675\tCalculates the cumulative distribution of a value within a window or partition.\n  1676\tThe cumulative distribution is defined as the number of rows preceding or\n  1677\tpeered with the current row divided by the total number of rows in the frame.\n  1678\t\n  1679\t``DenseRank``\n  1680\t-------------\n  1681\t\n  1682\t.. class:: DenseRank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1683\t\n  1684\tEquivalent to :class:`Rank` but does not have gaps.\n  1685\t\n  1686\t``FirstValue``\n  1687\t--------------\n  1688\t\n  1689\t.. class:: FirstValue(expression, **extra)\n  1690\t\n  1691\tReturns the value evaluated at the row that's the first row of the window\n  1692\tframe, or ``None`` if no such value exists.\n  1693\t\n  1694\t``Lag``\n  1695\t-------\n  1696\t\n  1697\t.. class:: Lag(expression, offset=1, default=None, **extra)\n  1698\t\n  1699\tCalculates the value offset by ``offset``, and if no row exists there, returns\n  1700\t``default``.\n  1701\t\n  1702\t``default`` must have the same type as the ``expression``, however, this is\n  1703\tonly validated by the database and not in Python.\n  1704\t\n  1705\t.. admonition:: MariaDB and ``default``\n  1706\t\n  1707\t    MariaDB `doesn't support <https://jira.mariadb.org/browse/MDEV-12981>`_\n  1708\t    the ``default`` parameter.\n  1709\t\n  1710\t``LastValue``\n  1711\t-------------\n  1712\t\n  1713\t.. class:: LastValue(expression, **extra)\n  1714\t\n  1715\tComparable to :class:`FirstValue`, it calculates the last value in a given\n  1716\tframe clause.\n  1717\t\n  1718\t``Lead``\n  1719\t--------\n  1720\t\n  1721\t.. class:: Lead(expression, offset=1, default=None, **extra)\n  1722\t\n  1723\tCalculates the leading value in a given :ref:`frame <window-frames>`. Both\n  1724\t``offset`` and ``default`` are evaluated with respect to the current row.\n  1725\t\n  1726\t``default`` must have the same type as the ``expression``, however, this is\n  1727\tonly validated by the database and not in Python.\n  1728\t\n  1729\t.. admonition:: MariaDB and ``default``\n  1730\t\n  1731\t    MariaDB `doesn't support <https://jira.mariadb.org/browse/MDEV-12981>`_\n  1732\t    the ``default`` parameter.\n  1733\t\n  1734\t``NthValue``\n  1735\t------------\n  1736\t\n  1737\t.. class:: NthValue(expression, nth=1, **extra)\n  1738\t\n  1739\tComputes the row relative to the offset ``nth`` (must be a positive value)\n  1740\twithin the window. Returns ``None`` if no row exists.\n  1741\t\n  1742\tSome databases may handle a nonexistent nth-value differently. For example,\n  1743\tOracle returns an empty string rather than ``None`` for character-based\n  1744\texpressions. Django doesn't do any conversions in these cases.\n  1745\t\n  1746\t``Ntile``\n  1747\t---------\n  1748\t\n  1749\t.. class:: Ntile(num_buckets=1, **extra)\n  1750\t\n  1751\tCalculates a partition for each of the rows in the frame clause, distributing\n  1752\tnumbers as evenly as possible between 1 and ``num_buckets``. If the rows don't\n  1753\tdivide evenly into a number of buckets, one or more buckets will be represented\n  1754\tmore frequently.\n  1755\t\n  1756\t``PercentRank``\n  1757\t---------------\n  1758\t\n  1759\t.. class:: PercentRank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1760\t\n  1761\tComputes the percentile rank of the rows in the frame clause. This\n  1762\tcomputation is equivalent to evaluating::\n  1763\t\n  1764\t    (rank - 1) / (total rows - 1)\n  1765\t\n  1766\tThe following table explains the calculation for the percentile rank of a row:\n  1767\t\n  1768\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1769\tRow # Value Rank Calculation  Percent Rank\n  1770\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1771\t1     15    1    (1-1)/(7-1)  0.0000\n  1772\t2     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1773\t3     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1774\t4     20    2    (2-1)/(7-1)  0.1666\n  1775\t5     30    5    (5-1)/(7-1)  0.6666\n  1776\t6     30    5    (5-1)/(7-1)  0.6666\n  1777\t7     40    7    (7-1)/(7-1)  1.0000\n  1778\t===== ===== ==== ============ ============\n  1779\t\n  1780\t``Rank``\n  1781\t--------\n  1782\t\n  1783\t.. class:: Rank(*expressions, **extra)\n  1784\t\n  1785\tComparable to ``RowNumber``, this function ranks rows in the window. The\n  1786\tcomputed rank contains gaps. Use :class:`DenseRank` to compute rank without\n  1787\tgaps.\n  1788\t\n  1789\t``RowNumber``\n  1790\t-------------\n  1791\t\n  1792\t.. class:: RowNumber(*expressions, **extra)\n  1793\t\n  1794\tComputes the row number according to the ordering of either the frame clause\n  1795\tor the ordering of the whole query if there is no partitioning of the\n  1796\t:ref:`window frame <window-frames>`.\n","message":"797 lines read from file starting from line 1000. \\\"\\\"\\\"Return a tzinfo instance with a fixed offset from UTC.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if isinstance(offset, timedelta):\\n        offset = offset.total_seconds() // 60\\n    sign = '-' if offset < 0 time,\\n# wrap the logic in a function and cache the result.\\n@functools.lru_cache()\\ndef get_default_timezone():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return the default time zone as a tzinfo instance.\\n\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return timezone.tzname(None) or str(timezone)\\n\\n# Timezone selection functions.\\n\\n# These functions don't change os.environ['TZ'] and call time.tzset()\\n# because it isn't thread safe.\\n\\n\\ndef activate(timezone):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a tzinfo subclass or a\\n    time zone name.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if isinstance(timezone, tzinfo):\\n        _active.value = timezone\\n    elif isinstance(timezone, str):\\n        _active.value = _get_timezone(timezone)\\n
