Temporarily set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    This is a context manager that uses django.utils.timezone.activate()\\n    to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone\\n    on exit.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a ``tzinfo`` subclass, a\\n    time zone name, or ``None``. If it is ``None``, Django enables the default\\n    time zone.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    def __init__(self, timezone):\\n        self.timezone = timezone\\n\\n    def __enter__(self):\\n        self.old_timezone = getattr(_active, 'value', None)\\n        if self.timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            activate(self.timezone)\\n\\n    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):\\n        if self.old_timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            _active.value = self.old_timezone\\n\\n\\n# Templates\\n\\ndef template_localtime(value, use_tz=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Check if value is a datetime and converts it to local time if necessary.\\n\\n    If use_tz is provided and is not None, that will force the value to\\n    be converted (or not), overriding the value of settings.USE_TZ.\\n\\n    This function is designed for use by the template engine.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    should_convert = (\\n        isinstance(value, datetime) and\\n        (settings.USE_TZ if use_tz is None else use_tz) and\\n        not is_naive(value) and\\n        getattr(value, 'convert_to_local_time', True)\\n    )\\n    return localtime(value) if should_convert else value\\n\\n\\n# Utilities\\n\\ndef localtime(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime.datetime to local time.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone\\n    is specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if value is None:\\n        value = now()\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"localtime() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef localdate(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime to local time and return the value's date.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone is\\n    specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return localtime(value, timezone).date()\\n\\n\\ndef now():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return an aware or naive datetime.datetime, depending on settings.USE_TZ.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return datetime.now(tz=utc if settings.USE_TZ else None)\\n\\n\\n# By design, these four functions don't perform any checks on their arguments.\\n# The caller should ensure that they don't receive an invalid value like None.\\n\\ndef is_aware(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is aware.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is not None\\n\\n\\ndef is_naive(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is naive.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is None\\n\\n\\ndef make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=...):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make a naive datetime.datetime in a given time zone aware.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # RemovedInDjango50Warning: is_dst parameter is deprecated.\\n    if is_dst is not ...:\\n        warnings.warn(\\n            'The is_dst argument to make_aware() is deprecated. Use '\\n            'datetime.datetime.replace(fold=...) instead.',\\n            RemovedInDjango50Warning,\\n            stacklevel=2,\\n        )\\n    if _is_pytz_zone(timezone):\\n        # This method is available for pytz time zones.\\n        if is_dst is ...:\\n            is_dst = None\\n        return timezone.localize(value, is_dst=is_dst)\\n    else:\\n        # Check that we won't overwrite the timezone of an aware datetime.\\n        if is_aware(value):\\n            raise ValueError(\\n                \\\"make_aware expects a naive datetime, got %s\\\" % value)\\n        # This may be wrong around DST changes!\\n        return value.replace(tzinfo=timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef make_naive(value, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make an aware datetime.datetime naive in a given time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"make_naive() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone).replace(tzinfo=None)\\n\\n\\ndef _datetime_ambiguous_or_imaginary(dt, tz):\\n    if _is_pytz_zone(tz):\\n        try:\\n            tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n        except (_pytz.AmbiguousTimeError, _pytz.NonExistentTimeError):\\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            return False\\n\\n    return tz.utcoffset(dt.replace(fold=not dt.fold)) != tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n\"}"},"extras":null}}}}}

For instance,\\n    the ``tzinfo`` argument of the standard datetime constructor doesn't work\\n    reliably for time zones with DST. Using UTC is generally safe; if you're\\n    using other time zones, you should review the `pytz`_ documentation\\n    carefully.\\n\\n.. note::\\n\\n    Python's :class:`datetime.time` objects also feature a ``tzinfo``\\n    attribute, and PostgreSQL has a matching ``time with time zone`` type.\\n    However, as PostgreSQL's docs put it, this type \\\"exhibits properties which\\n    lead to questionable usefulness\\\".\\n\\n    Django only supports naive time objects and will raise an exception if you\\n    attempt to save an aware time object, as a timezone for a time with no\\n    associated date does not make sense.\", \"new\": \".. warning::\\n\\n    Dealing with aware datetime objects isn't always intuitive. For instance,\\n    the ``tzinfo`` argument of the standard datetime constructor doesn't work\\n    reliably for time zones with DST. Using UTC is generally safe; if you're\\n    using other time zones, you should review the documentation for the\\n    timezone implementation you are using carefully.\\n\\n.. note::\\n\\n    Python's :class:`datetime.time` objects also feature a ``tzinfo``\\n    attribute, and PostgreSQL has a matching ``time with time zone`` type.\\n    However, as PostgreSQL's docs put it, this type \\\"exhibits properties which\\n    lead to questionable usefulness\\\".\\n\\n    Django only supports naive time objects and will raise an exception if you\\n    attempt to save an aware time object, as a timezone for a time with no\\n    associated date does not make sense.\"}]}"},"extras":null}}}}}

(or tomorrow)**\n   579\t\n   580\t   If you've always used naive datetimes, you probably believe that you can\n   581\t   convert a datetime to a date by calling its :meth:`~datetime.datetime.date`\n   582\t   method. You also consider that a :class:`~datetime.date` is a lot like a\n   583\t   :class:`~datetime.datetime`, except that it's less accurate.\n   584\t\n   585\t   None of this is true in a time zone aware environment::\n   586\t\n   587\t       >>> import datetime\n   588\t       >>> import pytz\n   589\t       >>> paris_tz = pytz.timezone(\"Europe/Paris\")\n   590\t       >>> new_york_tz = pytz.timezone(\"America/New_York\")\n   591\t       >>> paris = paris_tz.localize(datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30))\n   592\t       # This is the correct way to convert between time zones with pytz.\n   593\t       >>> new_york = new_york_tz.normalize(paris.astimezone(new_york_tz))\n   594\t       >>> paris == new_york, paris.date() == new_york.date()\n   595\t       (True, False)\n   596\t       >>> paris - new_york, paris.date() - new_york.date()\n   597\t       (datetime.timedelta(0), datetime.timedelta(1))\n   598\t       >>> paris\n   599\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 1, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   600\t       >>> new_york\n   601\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 2, 19, 30, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'America/New_York' EST-1 day, 19:00:00 STD>)\n   602\t\n   603\t   As this example shows, the same datetime has a different date, depending on\n   604\t   the time zone in which it is represented. But the real problem is more\n   605\t   fundamental.\n   606\t\n   607\t   A datetime represents a **point in time**. It's absolute: it doesn't depend\n   608\t   on anything. On the contrary, a date is a **calendaring concept**. It's a\n   609\t   period of time whose bounds depend on the time zone in which the date is\n   610\t   considered. As you can see, these two concepts are fundamentally different,\n   611\t   and converting a datetime to a date isn't a deterministic operation.\n   612\t\n   613\t   What does this mean in practice?\n   614\t\n   615\t   Generally, you should avoid converting a :class:`~datetime.datetime` to\n   616\t   :class:`~datetime.date`. For instance, you can use the :tfilter:`date`\n   617\t   template filter to only show the date part of a datetime. This filter will\n   618\t   convert the datetime into the current time zone before formatting it,\n   619\t   ensuring the results appear correctly.\n   620\t\n   621\t   If you really need to do the conversion yourself, you must ensure the\n   622\t   datetime is converted to the appropriate time zone first. You should review it\n   661\t   before attempting to manipulate aware datetimes.\n   662\t\n   663\t#. **How can I obtain the local time in the current time zone?**\n   664\t\n   665\t   Well, the first question is, do you really need to?\n   666\t\n   667\t   You should only use local time when you're interacting with humans, and the\n   668\t   template layer provides :ref:`filters and tags <time-zones-in-templates>`\n   669\t   to convert datetimes to the time zone of your choice.\n   670\t\n   671\t   Furthermore, Python knows how to compare aware datetimes, taking into\n   672\t   account UTC offsets when necessary. It's much easier (and possibly faster)\n   673\t   to write all your model and view code in UTC. So, in most circumstances,\n   674\t   the datetime in UTC returned by :func:`django.utils.timezone.now` will be\n   675\t   sufficient.\n   676\t\n   677\t   For the sake of completeness, though, if you really want the local time\n   678\t   in the current time zone, here's how you can obtain it::\n   679\t\n   680\t       >>> from django.utils import timezone\n   681\t       >>> timezone.localtime(timezone.now())\n   682\t       datetime.datetime(2012, 3, 3, 20, 10, 53, 873365, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'Europe/Paris' CET+1:00:00 STD>)\n   683\t\n   684\t   In this example, the current time zone is ``\"Europe/Paris\"``.\n   685\t\n   686\t#. **How can I see all available time zones?**\n   687\t\n   688\t   pytz_ provides helpers_, including a list of current time zones and a list\n   689\t   of all available time zones -- some of which are only of historical\n   690\t   interest. :mod:`zoneinfo` also provides similar functionality via\n   691\t   :func:`zoneinfo.available_timezones`.\n   692\t\n   693\t.. _pytz: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/\n   694\t.. _more examples: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#example-usage\n   695\t.. _these issues: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#problems-with-localtime\n   696\t.. _helpers: http://pytz.sourceforge.net/#helpers\n   697\t.. _tz database: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tz_database\n","message":"48 lines read from file starting from line 650. Install backports.zoneinfo or '\\n            'set USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ to True to use pytz instead.'\\n        )\\n    return zoneinfo\\n\\n\\ndef _get_timezone(key):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return a timezone instance by name.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if getattr(settings, 'USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ', False):\\n        pytz = _get_pytz()\\n        return pytz.timezone(key)\\n    if zoneinfo is None:\\n        raise ImportError(\\n            'zoneinfo is not available. Install backports.zoneinfo or '\\n            'set USE_DEPRECATED_PYTZ to True to use pytz instead.'\\n        )\\n    return zoneinfo.ZoneInfo(key)\\n\\n\\ndef get_fixed_timezone(offset):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return a tzinfo instance with a fixed offset from UTC.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if isinstance(offset, timedelta):\\n        offset = offset.total_seconds() // 60\\n    sign = '-' if offset < 0 else '+'\\n    hhmm = '%02d%02d' % divmod(abs(offset), 60)\\n    name = sign + hhmm\\n    return timezone(timedelta(minutes=offset), name)\\n\\n\\n# In order to avoid accessing settings at compile time,\\n# wrap the logic in a function and cache the result.\\n@functools.lru_cache()\\ndef get_default_timezone():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return the default time zone as a tzinfo instance.\\n\\n    This is the time zone defined by settings.TIME_ZONE.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return _get_timezone(settings.TIME_ZONE)\\n\\n\\n# This function exists for consistency with _get_timezone_name(get_default_timezone())\\n\\n\\n_active = Local()\\n\\n\\ndef get_current_timezone():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the currently active time zone as a tzinfo instance.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return getattr(_active, \\\"value\\\", get_default_timezone())\\n\\n\\ndef get_current_timezone_name():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Return the name of the currently active time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return _get_timezone_name(get_current_timezone())\\n\\n\\ndef _get_timezone_name(timezone):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return the offset for fixed offset timezones, or the name of timezone if\\n    not set.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return timezone.tzname(None) or str(timezone)\\n\\n# Timezone selection functions.\\n\\n# These functions don't change os.environ['TZ'] and call time.tzset()\\n# because it isn't thread safe.\\n\\n\\ndef activate(timezone):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a tzinfo subclass or a\\n    time zone name.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if isinstance(timezone, tzinfo):\\n        _active.value = timezone\\n    elif isinstance(timezone, str):\\n        _active.value = _get_timezone(timezone)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"Invalid timezone: %r\\\" % timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef deactivate():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Unset the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    Django will then use the time zone defined by settings.TIME_ZONE.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if hasattr(_active, \\\"value\\\"):\\n        del _active.value\\n\\n\\nclass override(ContextDecorator):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Temporarily set the time zone for the current thread.\\n\\n    This is a context manager that uses django.utils.timezone.activate()\\n    to set the timezone on entry and restores the previously active timezone\\n    on exit.\\n\\n    The ``timezone`` argument must be an instance of a ``tzinfo`` subclass, a\\n    time zone name, or ``None``. If it is ``None``, Django enables the default\\n    time zone.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    def __init__(self, timezone):\\n        self.timezone = timezone\\n\\n    def __enter__(self):\\n        self.old_timezone = getattr(_active, 'value', None)\\n        if self.timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            activate(self.timezone)\\n\\n    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):\\n        if self.old_timezone is None:\\n            deactivate()\\n        else:\\n            _active.value = self.old_timezone\\n\\n\\n# Templates\\n\\ndef template_localtime(value, use_tz=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Check if value is a datetime and converts it to local time if necessary.\\n\\n    If use_tz is provided and is not None, that will force the value to\\n    be converted (or not), overriding the value of settings.USE_TZ.\\n\\n    This function is designed for use by the template engine.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    should_convert = (\\n        isinstance(value, datetime) and\\n        (settings.USE_TZ if use_tz is None else use_tz) and\\n        not is_naive(value) and\\n        getattr(value, 'convert_to_local_time', True)\\n    )\\n    return localtime(value) if should_convert else value\\n\\n\\n# Utilities\\n\\ndef localtime(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime.datetime to local time.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone\\n    is specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if value is None:\\n        value = now()\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"localtime() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef localdate(value=None, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Convert an aware datetime to local time and return the value's date.\\n\\n    Only aware datetimes are allowed. When value is omitted, it defaults to\\n    now().\\n\\n    Local time is defined by the current time zone, unless another time zone is\\n    specified.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return localtime(value, timezone).date()\\n\\n\\ndef now():\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Return an aware or naive datetime.datetime, depending on settings.USE_TZ.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return datetime.now(tz=utc if settings.USE_TZ else None)\\n\\n\\n# By design, these four functions don't perform any checks on their arguments.\\n# The caller should ensure that they don't receive an invalid value like None.\\n\\ndef is_aware(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is aware.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is not None\\n\\n\\ndef is_naive(value):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Determine if a given datetime.datetime is naive.\\n\\n    The concept is defined in Python's docs:\\n    https://docs.python.org/library/datetime.html#datetime.tzinfo\\n\\n    Assuming value.tzinfo is either None or a proper datetime.tzinfo,\\n    value.utcoffset() implements the appropriate logic.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    return value.utcoffset() is None\\n\\n\\ndef make_aware(value, timezone=None, is_dst=...):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make a naive datetime.datetime in a given time zone aware.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # RemovedInDjango50Warning: is_dst parameter is deprecated.\\n    if is_dst is not ...:\\n        warnings.warn(\\n            'The is_dst argument to make_aware() is deprecated. Use '\\n            'datetime.datetime.replace(fold=...) instead.',\\n            RemovedInDjango50Warning,\\n            stacklevel=2,\\n        )\\n    if _is_pytz_zone(timezone) or hasattr(timezone, 'localize'):\\n        # pytz zones have a localize method which handles DST correctly.\\n        if is_dst is ...:\\n            is_dst = None\\n        return timezone.localize(value, is_dst=is_dst)\\n    else:\\n        # Check that we won't overwrite the timezone of an aware datetime.\\n        if is_aware(value):\\n            raise ValueError(\\n                \\\"make_aware expects a naive datetime, got %s\\\" % value)\\n        # This may be wrong around DST changes!\\n        return value.replace(tzinfo=timezone)\\n\\n\\ndef make_naive(value, timezone=None):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"Make an aware datetime.datetime naive in a given time zone.\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    if timezone is None:\\n        timezone = get_current_timezone()\\n    # Emulate the behavior of astimezone() on Python < 3.6.\\n    if is_naive(value):\\n        raise ValueError(\\\"make_naive() cannot be applied to a naive datetime\\\")\\n    return value.astimezone(timezone).replace(tzinfo=None)\\n\\n\\ndef _datetime_ambiguous_or_imaginary(dt, tz):\\n    if _is_pytz_zone(tz) or hasattr(tz, 'localize'):\\n        try:\\n            tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n        except Exception:\\n            # pytz raises AmbiguousTimeError or NonExistentTimeError\\n            return True\\n        else:\\n            return False\\n\\n    return tz.utcoffset(dt.replace(fold=not dt.fold)) != tz.utcoffset(dt)\\n\"}"},"extras":null}}}}}

If ``timezone`` is set to ``None``, it\\n    defaults to the :ref:`current time zone <default-current-time-zone>`.\\n\\n    When using ``pytz``, the ``pytz.AmbiguousTimeError`` exception is raised if\\n    you try to make ``value`` aware during a DST transition where the same time\\n    occurs twice (when reverting from DST). For example, you can use ``Trunc`` to calculate\n   512\tthe number of sales per day.\n   513\t\n   514\t``Trunc`` takes a single ``expression``, representing a ``DateField``,\n   515\t``TimeField``, or ``DateTimeField``, a ``kind`` representing a date or time\n   516\tpart, and an ``output_field`` that's either ``DateTimeField()``,\n   517\t``TimeField()``, or ``DateField()``.
